lines.st: And so Kereseira, the singer of epics, the gentlest hero of Agamem,
Hung her harp upon the branches of the laurel tree—
Its body made from the tallest firs atop Mount Hymnoi, its strings made from the bravest burdenbeast that roamed the Agnes Plains;
When it played, all things in the Land of Heroes sang together, riding upon the winds in freedom.
Now, Kereseira hangs her harp by the laurels.
'The outlanders have come from afar,' said Kereseira, clutching her knife tightly,
'They trample our lands, set fire to our homes, and let the rivers cease their peaceful flow.
And they command all things to sing for them!
This alone wrenches my heart.
My harp sings only the tales of the heroes!'
—Kerespereia

lines.x: 'While there is no specific mention in the records, historians generally agree that Padishah Malla most favored Athenius out of the three great nomadic cities that Sargon built for Minos.'
'Sargonian and Minoan engineers took great pains to preserve its signature feature as the Mountain-Rimmed Garden, when it was moved to a nomadic platform.'
'Today, you will see the same mountains and rivers as Kereseira once did, upon entering Athenius through the mountain pass that Padishah Malla made it a point to preserve.'
'This is a route that I highly recommend my readers—'
Oh, shut up! Just look at where your stupid book has led us!
And this big, heavy piece of stupid crap we have to carry, just the two of us!
Take it easy, calm down! No hitting!
Like I wasn't tired enough after walking for days on end...
You're one to talk, girl!
Why did you decide, on a whim, to travel separately from the group? I would be lying in the Athenian flower fields and drinking floral liqueur by now, if it wasn't for you!
It wasn't on a whim.
I told you, my father used to tell stories about a place called Minos on the other side of the Agnes River, stories that left a deep impression on me.
And I told you it was an invaluable opportunity for diplomacy with Minos. I wanted to go to Athenius via Kereseira's old route and experience history—

You didn't have to experience history with this thing! It's got to be, what, over fifty kilos, with just the two of us to carry it? Are you trying to experience the Yanese idiom where you bear a bundle of logs to beg forgiveness?
It's a bundle of 'thorns'. The Yanese wouldn't appreciate you rewriting their idioms.
That's not the point! Have you got anything in that skull of yours besides history books?
Alright, alright, no hitting. I wasn't comfortable letting someone else handle it.
This is the most valuable item in the Varjavandabad Museum's collection, an important relic to both Sargon and Minos. We came all this way...
To give it back to the Minoans, to build a new era of friendship between the two nations. So you haven't forgotten your mission. Yay. Clap clap.
YOU, ARE, LOST! You are LATE! And you have MISSED your appointment with the Astynomos of Athenius, Medjehtiqedti Nasutu Mashayer!
Uh... well, the rest of the mission should be in the city by now, using the normal route. I'm sure Behnui will explain to the Astynomos.
Behnui? That noble who thinks his scat smells like roses? Are you just acting dumb, or are you actually that dumb?
Did you see that look on his face when he learned he was gonna be your assistant in the mission? It's like you've stolen fifty carts of Pure Gold from him.
No, no, Behnui is a very nice man.
Taking on such an important mission so soon after I became the curator of Varjavandabad Museum made me... anyway, Behnui helped a lot on this journey.
Fine, suit yourself. I'm just tagging along as a tourist.
Let's get going. Before we collapse from exhaustion.
Yes, yes. See? We're almost there.
Look—
Wow—
Wow—
I've read a lot about Athenius, but to actually find myself here...
I see fields of flowers... floral liqueur and flower cakes! We're saved!
Minos, the land of a thousand poleis, separated from Sargon by a river.
Here is Athenius, the First Polis, the Mountain-Rimmed Garden.
As the city that Titi has only ever known through historical records reveals itself, she lays eyes on a scene very different from the sands of Sargon.
White walls.
Green fields.
Rivers weaving their way through mountains.
Strange temples unabashedly announce themselves on the mountops and beside the rivers. If heroic epics are the rain that nourishes Minos, then these temples are the mushrooms that grow afterwards.
No wonder this land has birthed so many famous plays, philosophers, stories, and legends, which not even the sands could bury.
Hey, why are you pushing me?
I haven't even caught my breath yet...
For days, Titi has been placating her Beast Aristocrat companion, trying to wear a mask of calm for this journey.
Now, though, she is even more eager than Mio to get into this city.
You see the ferry crossing down there? We're not truly in the city until we cross over.
Hurry, now.
Should I help?
The river is freezing. I see the ice floating on it... but all you have is an oar.
Just sit tight, Ithakos.
My luggage is not heavy. I can walk on my own.
Let me ferry you for a little bit... there's little else I can do for you.
It's been two years since you experienced divine revelation, since our meeting.
You taught us the knowledge you brought from the cities, built a village on the marsh that the Sargonians wouldn't bother with, farming the paddy fields and fending off the cold.
We lived together. We were family.
For the past two years, I've been trying to figure out why divine revelation led me here.
Now I understand... the Sargonians will appear on the riverbanks nearby. I can reach the place quickly by following this river out of the marshes.
...I see.
Do you believe that the guerrillas can defeat the Sargonians?
Even though we've been losing for a century?
(Nods)
I haven't received any divine revelations, and I can't tell the future. I can't even leave with you.
But I will promise what I can.
I'll protect our village, our houses, our farms and flowerbeds. Perhaps we can go to Athenius, where you came from, once you're victorious.

I'll still be a ferryman, ferrying you to your workplace at the temple on the other side of the city as the sun shines upon the Delphi Canal... I've been imagining the scene ever since you described it to me.
...Are you listening, Ithakos?
Minos is in for a cold winter this year, brother.
(Rows furiously)
The ferryman shakes his head. What was his response to Ithakos?
For many years, he still could not remember.
There you are, Master Xanthos. You're ten minutes late.
I've never been late before, Lykeion. This is the first time.

I saw your chiton by the shore. I thought you might have dropped it yesterday when you left, or a thief might have stolen it only to decide it was too old and ragged to keep. Little could I have imagined that you had gone for a morning swim.
So, out of habit, I folded and stowed your chiton—
You cannot reproach me for it, for you had not informed me of your schedule.
It is I who should reproach you, for the doctor has warned you to be careful...
Thank you, Lykeion. Over the years, I have taught you how to fight and ferry, and you have handled my daily needs wholeheartedly.
I am not reproaching you. I simply have to leave early today, so tardiness weighs heavily on my mind.
You're changing the topic again... wait, are you going to the cemetery to visit...?
Ithakos, my friend.
He was only your age when we bid farewell. I never saw him again.
I should say some words of comfort, but I find them beyond my ability.
You should stay within your abilities. It's rarely your place to go beyond them.
The fruit farmers and workers at the First Temple have been in conflict over boat assignments for days. They're asking the dockmaster—me—to arbitrate. This is what we should be putting our minds to.
Let's go to the main crossing. It will be a busy day.
One moment, Master Xanthos.
The Astynomos 'requests' your presence at this secondary crossing today, to repair the boats that have broken down.
No one in Athenius uses the word 'priority' more than Lydia. What else did she say?
Nothing.
Is the Sargonian mission due to arrive today?
...I think so.
Hmph. Should I be grateful or angry that Lydia still remembers me?
She should pull all the teeth out of my oars, or simply throw me in jail, so that I never get the chance to meet the Sargonian envoys coming to visit their old 'stomping grounds'.
*pant* *pant* Excuse me! Can we get a ride across?
Too many people at the other crossings. We'll be late.
You said your ambassador has been delayed by... certain circumstances?
I mean no offense, as that is precisely the case.
I beg your understanding, Astynomos. The Minoan vistas are beautiful, while Her Excellency is young and curious. She is bound to commit some... youthful indiscretions.
I say again, as deputy chief of mission, and as the man invested with the titles of the Ancient and Exalted Sargonian House of Enshi-Pah, I am equally empowered to act on the Empire's behalf.
Of course. I have the utmost confidence in you, Kýrios Behnui.
When do the Athenians intend to hold the ceremony, then?
What ceremony?
The exchange ceremony.

Our two nations formally established diplomatic relations at the beginning of this year. As part of our cultural exchange, we are repatriating our relics of Kereseira, the founding hero of Minos, to Athenius.
In exchange, we will receive the technical legacy that the Sargonians left behind during the construction of Athenius, Corinia, and Lachedamon... surely you have not forgotten, Honored Astynomos?
Of course not... Your Excellency.
We stand now in the Astynomos' Office, instead of... elsewhere... because of the non-aggression pact that was signed following the last major battle between our two nations in the Minoan borderlands.
Hmph...
Sorry, I have been rather overworked lately. I'm sure you saw the vast construction sites and passionate craftsmen as you passed by the agora by the river when you entered the city.
Yes.
It's the rebuilding of the First Temple. One in five Athenians are involved in the project.
The First Temple...?
As you said yourself, Kereseira is the founding hero of Minos. Perhaps you've heard of her deeds.
At the very beginning, she led refugees from scattered tribes out of war and plague, and brought them to a garden encircled by mountains.
Those who followed Kereseira built the First Temple of Minos here, and thus began the bloodline of heroes.
After that came Athenius, and the land of a thousand poleis.
That First Temple was lost to the fires of war. Now, it is being rebuilt by the people of Athenius themselves... I'm sure the significance is not lost on you.
...I understand.
To obtain the stone needed to rebuild the temple, however, we've kept Athenius by Mt. Hymnoi for an entire week.
And it's apple season. Fresh apples need to be ferried out through the crossings, while unripe fruits need plenty of sunlight.
The quality of the apples will be compromised if the city does not move to an area with more sunlight soon. I'm sure you saw the fruit farmers gathered at the crossing.
Yes, I did.

The people need to commemorate their heroes. The farmers need to ensure that their harvest is not ruined. You understand that an Astynomos does not have all that much power in the Minoan administrative system.
I need the priests' approval for everything.
I confess that I am rather overwhelmed right now.
Perhaps looking for excuses has eaten up too much of your time.
Your Excellency misunderstands.
I'm of the younger generation myself. At a time when all the centrum powers are actively seeking change, I believe smaller nations like ours must keep up with the times.
The exchange of Kereseira's relics for the data from the Three Poleis has been at least six months in the making.
It is an honor to play a small part in facilitating the new friendship between Sargon and Minos after years of conflict.
Hmph. We'll see about that.
I suggest you and your mission take some time to see the city. The Athenian sights are spectacular, and I'm sure you'll find it well worth your time.
Perhaps you could even visit the site of the First Temple's rebuilding... after all, it is Kereseira's relics that you are returning. Maybe this was ordained by fate.
Minoans love drama and philosophy. We enjoy the rigor of preparation and logical deduction, but we also hope for pleasant surprises.
As for the exchange, we will hold a formal ceremony in a couple of days.
By Minoan tradition, ceremonial occasions begin with a play directed by the priests, imploring the heroes to bless the occasion and ensure that everything goes smoothly.
However, Athenius created the post of Astynomos two years ago to reduce the burden on the priests and simplify procedures. Therefore, I've already assigned someone to begin preparations.
My trusty aide and Grammateús here will make comprehensive arrangements for you during your stay. Seek him out if you have any issues... Hekádemos!
Here.
Hekádemos, Nobody of Minos, the Blaring Megaphone, at your service... during working hours.
I have no use for some pretty boy.
I mean, it's been a long journey and I am tired. I will wait at the hotel until the arrangements for the exchange ceremony are made.
As for visiting the First Temple rebuilding site, I will leave the opportunity to Her Excellency the Ambassador. She is a museologist, full of energy. I'm sure she'll be very interested.
I see. I will have Hekádemos speak to Ambassador Medjehtiqedti directly.
I look forward to what 'pleasant surprise' you have to offer, Astynomos.
But—I hope I don't learn you are stalling for time, or hiding some ulterior motive.
I could say the same to you, Kýrios Behnui.
Look out the window. The morning mist on the Delphi Canal is just beginning to dissipate. This peace wasn't easy to win. I hope we can all be honest with each other.
What a shame that I didn't have the opportunity to see Kyría Mashayer today. Young and promising, yet mercurial... what is she like?
Ugh—
Oh, purr-lease. You've only just put one foot on the boat. Look at you.
(Whisper) It's tipping!
It's a boat. It's supposed to do that.
Didn't you say you signed up for the yacht club when you studied in Max D.C.?
(Whisper) I-I was too busy with my studies. Most of my time was spent either in the restoration room, or at archaeological sites... I only ended up going there twice.
So you're from Columbia. I had thought, by your garb, that you were...
Master Xanthos.
What?
She's boatsick.
A rare occurrence.
I-I'm not. M-My legs are just tired from all the walking and buckled a little...
What do we do?
Take the luggage onto the boat. I'll go and get something.
Take her into the cabin. The waves are not strong today and the boat should sail steady. The Delphi Canal treats those with good intentions kindly.
We'll go to the main crossing once we've delivered her, to see that Sargonian ambassador... can't let the Astynomos' heads-up go to waste, can we?
I'll come with you, Master Xanthos. I must keep an eye on you.
...Bring the passenger's luggage first.
...They weren't listening to me.
(Whisper) You're missing the point! They've got a vendetta against you!
(Whisper) It's not too late to switch your nationality to Columbian! Don't—
Please be careful. This is very important.

Hey— You—
Forget it. Let's get on the boat.
It's really heavy. What is it?
It's...
Movie figurines from Wrankwood that we're planning to sell here. We've even got ones from the latest hit, Lady Dupont.
But there are no cinemas in Athenius.
That's the point. You want to sell shoes, you do it in a country where everybody is barefoot.
Did you say 'the Delphi Canal will treat those with good intentions kindly', Master Ferryman?
(Nods)
The source of the Delphi River is on Mt. Hymnoi, flowing through the plains between the Minoan mountains, past the former sites of Athenius and Corinia from before they became nomadic cities...
It helped our warriors fend off the iron hooves of the Sargonian raiders.
It has been turned into a canal now, but it continues to nurture singers and debaters, and the culture of Minos.
Yes, the Delphi Canal will treat those with good intentions kindly... except Sargonians.
Perhaps there are Sargonians who understand and love Minoan culture.
The last Sargonian who introduced herself with those words was the sister of the Shahanshah, Malla, Queen of the Far Shore.
She burned every Minoan city but the Three Poleis to the ground, closed the temples, banned the plays, imported Sargonians, and assimilated Minoan culture in every way possible.
She brought the darkest age upon our country. She showed her 'understanding' and 'love' by bleeding the heroic blood of the Minoans dry.
Is this 'ambassador' from far away another shameless noble who seeks to be the next Queen of the Far Shore by trampling Minos underfoot?
Listen—
Hey, is it tipping again—
Ugh—
Whoa, eek! Ugh—!
Help—!
*sigh*...
Hm...
Never mind all that. Have an apple. It'll settle your stomach.
So you had gone to get an apple.
Th-Thanks.
Keep your eyes closed and don't look at the water. You'll feel better that way.
I'll be fine. I want to see it.
Leaning against the side of the boat, Titi forces her eyes open.
The sun is hanging high above. The morning mist of the Delphi Canal in autumn has completely dissipated, and the last cool touch of the wind brushes against her cheeks.
I know the blade of vengeance and the decapitation of the conqueror are core themes of Minoan drama.
But I've seen more thought-provoking expressions as well.
I will see each and every one clearly with my own eyes.
I came to Athenius to—
To what?
Look there...
Even though I feel like I'm gonna throw up all my internal organs, I still...
This river and this city... they're so beautiful...
I think I'll love this place.
I am honored to hear you say as much.
We're here.
I wish you a joyful and safe trip in Minos.
I've been dreaming about the guerrillas again, Kassandra.
This is the third time you've come to Endymion Temple to ask for a dream reading, Megas.
You still dream of that night seven years ago?
Yes...
We hid in the forest on that dark night. The Sargonians surrounded the wood, hunting for us.
We could not make a sound, let alone risk a light. We had to disassemble our weapons in pitch darkness.
Screws, springs, Originium components... I tied the parts of my Arts Unit to my clothes and shoes, disguised as accessories.
The fear had me so cold that I did not notice when an Originium component had cut my palm open. I thought the wet stuff was sweat.
When the sun rose, we walked past the Sargonians, disguised as lumberjacks from a nearby village, about a dozen in total. We'd be dead if they saw through our disguise.
They sized us up. For a moment there, I thought the game was up. In my panic, I tripped over a tree root and plunged into the mud, face-first.
A Sargonian laughed and rapped on my shoulder with the hilt of his sword, disdainfully. But I was happy. I knew we were safe.
I keep dreaming about it. Do you think...?
You want to know if it's a divine revelation?
Last year, I saw with my own eyes how the heroes spoke their will through you on the Agora.
You are the speaker of the heroes of Minos, and the only priest in Athenius who can tell a divine revelation from a normal dream.
If the gods will that I take up arms again, then I will... no matter how much I fear...
Please sit down. Your legs are trembling and your face is pale.
The heroes of Minos will not force their children back into a nightmare when they have already seen far too much of the horrors of war.
You mean...?
I think you have misinterpreted the revelation.

Wisdom and courage shine upon you and your friends in place of the twin moons who closed their eyes, and delivered you in the morn, just as you woke up from dream to greet the beautiful Athenian dawn...
The virtues of the heroes should be praised, but the pain that has lingered for seven years has blinded you to the beautiful fruit that has ripened on the branches of fate...
Pick it, for it is the heroes' blessing upon you.
A blessing... on an ordinary Minoan like me?
Our gods and heroes were once ordinary Minoans.
Thank you, Kassandra. I'm beginning to understand...
I have come to relieve you, Kassandra.
It's already time? So many visitors to the temple today.

You have so much on your plate, not just with visitors to Endymion Temple, but also acting on behalf of the head priest of Kereseira's First Temple in administrative duties while the position remains vacant.
There's no need to worry about me, Folia. It is my calling to interpret revelation and speak on behalf of the gods.
So, you should know better than I do that the man's dream was mundane, not a divine revelation. To use more Columbian words—
He may have post-traumatic stress disorder. I know.
Why didn't you tell him?
Divine revelation should be an honor for any Minoan, but there were only shame and fear in his eyes.
He wants to receive guidance from the gods, but he fears that the nightmare is truly a revelation that he cannot refuse.
If he believes in my interpretation more than he believes in himself, then I have a responsibility... to make his life a little easier, if nothing else.
I'll prepare some herbal tea with a soothing effect for him, and ask a doctor to follow up.
You have the afternoon off, don't you? You should stop spending it on the temple and focus on yourself.
Thank you, Folia. You take so much weight off my shoulders.
I'm so very sorry, Hekádemos.
I dozed off as soon as I reached the hotel. I didn't realize I would make you wait for a full three hours...
There's no need to worry, Kyría Mashayer. Waiting is part of the job for the Astynomos' Grammateús.
You still look weak. Are you sure you want to visit the First Temple right now? You can rest a little longer.
Yes, I'm sure! If anything will bring me back to full strength right now, it's Kereseira's First Temple!
I understand the original site of the temple was where Kereseira, the founding hero of Minos, first received divine revelation in the mountains.
The first temple that she built there became the genesis of Minos.
When Sargon... invaded Minos, they rebuilt the three great poleis as nomadic cities, but Kereseira's war-torn First Temple remained where it was.
Now, after a century, Athenius is rebuilding this temple, giving me the opportunity to see the historical building in its original form! This is incredibly exciting!
You are surprisingly well-versed in Minoan history... and surprisingly honest.
Unfortunately, the original plans of the First Temple have been lost, and thus cannot be perfectly recreated.
However, there's quite a bit of 'classical' tradition in the way that they're building the temple.
What do you mean?
'To revive the Minoan spirit, we must emulate the heroes who built Athenius, and build the temple of our own accord.'
Before the referendum took place, these words by the original proponent moved everyone's hearts, and the vote on rebuilding the temple passed almost unanimously.
No engineering blueprint, no staffing. Kereseira's First Temple shall be built through the passion of the Minoan people, and the veneration of the heroes that flows in their veins.
Wow! A living example of the Minoan spontaneousness that has driven so much artistic achievement!
Thank you. I hope it's completed soon...
Rebuilding in the classical style... how interesting! The proponent of the rebuilding must be an ambitious hero beloved by the people!
Uh... how can I describe that Forte lady?
(Why is he taking so much time to think? Did you ask a question you shouldn't have?)
(Even if I did, I'm sure Hekádemos won't mind! He looks so nice, after all. Maybe he's choosing his words carefully, with a Minoan love of poetry.)
Uh...
Give me three seconds, Kyría Mashayer.
O-Okay.

Three, two, one...
Alright!
You've thought of the answer?
No, it's now five o'clock, and my shift is over.
Huh?
I'll answer your question tomorrow, when I'm back at work. Enjoy your visit.
But... we aren't even at the First Temple yet!
See those scaffolds? This street is a stone's throw from the main entrance to the temple. I'm sure you can find your own way.
Wait—
Now, if you'll excuse me, I have a drinking engagement with a friend. He'll kill me if I'm late. Goodbye.
You know, I was worried that the Astynomos' Grammateús would just take me around to see mundane tourist sites, leaving me with nothing to talk about when we get back.
You've got a way of smoothing things over for people, you know that?
If you ask me, I think the deferential-looking Liberi kid just enjoyed a three-hour break!
Well, no need to complain. There's plenty of time. Let's check out the surrounding area.
This is the land of heroes, after all. Maybe we'll walk out the door and straight into a heroic epic!
I knew it! You came looking for adventure!
Rats, I came to you when I left the Grand Bazaar because I wanted a sugar mommy with money. Little did I know you were such a troublemaker...
Hey! Wait for me!
Caught you!
Keep your distance.
That's no way to treat your old priest school classmate! I remember you were much more passionate back when we participated in debate competitions at Theros Temple.
Quiet down already. Loudmouth.
Looks like you know that I've come to debate you?
Don't worry, if you lose, I'll sing your defeat through a megaphone in standard hexameter.
Besides, you're the one who gave me the nickname 'Blaring Megaphone'. In return, I called you—
Eloquent Rock! Blaring Megaphone!
So glad the two great Athenian debaters are here! We're saved!
Come with me! The apple farmers are making a scene in the temple, saying that the construction workers are delaying the quarrying to ruin this year's harvest by keeping the apples from sunlight.
What about Pallas? The people may have chosen to build the temple of their own accord, but she was the initial proponent. Why don't you talk to her to resolve your disputes?
Pallas has been crazy busy lately. No one's seen her since last night. Maybe someone else needed her.
When people heard about it, they were all excited to emulate the heroes in building the temple, doing as much or as little work anyone had time for.
Who could have thought that things would get so chaotic, even delaying the city's move to sunnier climes?
Please, find us a solution through debate... or at least find us a loser!
I guess the two of us in a room naturally drives people to think of debate...
That's why I said—keep your distance, and keep quiet.
How was I supposed to know? Just because you're smart doesn't mean you should skip the explanation and go straight to the conclusion!
Actually, no, that's not right. I DID come to debate you. Everything works out!
I'm busy.
Always the same excuse after all these years. How on Terra did you win that debate championship with such a bland tongue?
Thank you.
You won't muddle your way out of this. For years, you've been running before I can finish the word 'debate'. Don't forget that there's still a competition we haven't settled!
Come on, who are you arguing for? The fruit farmers or the temple workers?
You've known me long enough to know that I don't lie.
*sigh*... I'm going to the new Oripathy clinic to get medicine. Can you move?
What...?
Oripathy medicine? Didn't he pick up some last Wednesday...? Is he getting worse?
I need to find out.
Hey! Don't go!
The fruit farmers and our guys are about to come to blows! Who's gonna solve our problem? We can't just pull some random soul off the street!
Look, Mio! So many people here in the temple! Looks like the Athenians are really excited to rebuild it! I feel like joining in myself!
Excuse me, sir. Are you looking for someone to help?
Are you moving or not?!
No!
Are you moving or not?!
NO!
Wh-What a passionate debate! I can feel the heroic blood in their voices!
...But they're not actually saying anything.
They're just yelling at the top of their lungs. I don't even know what they're saying.
The dispute over Athenius's route has been brewing between the fruit farmers and the temple builders for ages.
Embarrassing, really, to make a random tourist off the street try to solve our dispute. Heck, even I couldn't tell half of what they're saying...
You said you wanted to know what they're saying...
Stop looking for trouble, Titi.
This 'random tourist off the street' has an idea.
Hey! Don't just wander in there! Things could get rough!

The craftsmen rep and the militiaman arguing on behalf of the fruit farmers are not budging in the slightest, their faces red with anger. They fail to notice the pair of pointy black ears poking out from beneath the stone table.
The tiny Feline girl leaps out and grabs their hands with a grin, bringing them together in a handshake.
Caught off guard, the two reps lose their balance and topple onto the stone table, staring at the jolly intruder.
Hey, who brought this girl to the temple? This is no place for children.
See, you're so unreasonable, even an outlander is embarrassed. You're a disgrace to Athenius!
Stop adding fuel to the fire!
Listen, girl, this is Minoan business. We don't need some foreigner telling us how to make up.
Who said anything about making up?
Whuh?
The only way to solve a problem is to face it directly. How long do you think the peace would last, even if you made up here and now?
Besides, when in Minos, do as the Minoans do. The heroic epics and fantastical stories of Minos are full of ancient wisdom. You don't need me to tell you how to solve your problems.
They say the mere mention of Heraces, the greatest Minoan warrior, sent enemies fleeing in terror.
But he never raised a hand against his fellow Minoans. If someone insulted him, he would challenge them to arm-wrestling.
Settle it with a contest of strength, eh? Sounds good! Spares us a sore throat.
I'm not done yet.
There is another legend about a silent hero, who was once a bold and passionate youth, surrounded by flowers and adoration.
As fate would have it, he learned a forbidden secret, knowledge that no one should possess, so he kept silent for thirty years.
Wait, we know the legend of Heraces, but who's this 'silent hero'? Did you just make him up on the spot?
Oh, it's a Minoan tale that my father used to tell me. It's rather obscure, but it's a really good parable—
Ahem. Anyway, I propose that you follow in the footsteps of ancient Minoans, and settle this with an arm-wrestling match.
But with one extra condition—you only get to talk if you win, no matter how pissed you might be.
Never heard that rule before.
Are you poor with words, or weak of limb?
Enough talk. Let's do it.
Alright! The inaugural First Temple Arm-Wrestling Tournament is on. Ready, set—
Go!
Well, well, well! The militiaman looks lean, but he's got skill, and he takes round one when the big guy loses focus for a second!
You—
Shut it! Loser doesn't get to talk!

New apples can't get out with all the stone piled up at the crossing! The orchards are all in the mountain's shadow, and the apples that haven't ripened yet don't get any sun! The whole harvest's going to the dumps at this rate!
One-nil. Round two.
Hey, all those muscles ain't for nothing! Looks like hauling marble is good exercise!
We can't move the stone until the apples are out of the way! Besides, the building of the First Temple was inspired by the Minoan heroes! Who's gonna take responsibility if the work's delayed? You?
One-all. Next!
Apple harvesting and hauling stone... I see.
Minoans sure are fun to watch! But things will get less interesting when they start getting tired...
Hey, why did they stop?
They've realized the arm-wrestling isn't about determining a victor, but giving them an opportunity to hear each other out.
When you're angry, your ears stop working, and even simple things become complicated, much less long-standing disputes.
You're more thoughtful than you look.
But how do you solve the core problem, now that you've gotten emotion out of the way?
*pant*... *pant*...
*pant*... *pant*...
Looks like a draw.
Young lady...
Call me Titi.
It's not like we screwed the fruit farmers on purpose, Titi.

We've got a lot of hands in the rebuilding, but they only quarry stone when they please, where they please. I brought fifteen tons of marble down the mountain by myself yesterday, and it's still far from enough.
Look, we understand. But the apples don't care.
Increasing quarrying efficiency, while getting enough sun for the apples... why don't we meet in the middle, then?
What do you mean?
Just remove the peak that's blocking the sunlight.
Hahahaha! Some 'middle'! That's the craziest thing I've ever heard!
It is intriguing, though.
Out of personal curiosity, I studied the urban planning and natural environment of Athenius on my way here.
The peak that's in the way of the sunlight is only a secondary peak, with soft soil at the top and a large marble layer underneath—it would make an excellent quarry.
You mean we can get sunlight to the orchards by leveling the peak, without having to change the city's route? Is that even possible?
The fruit farmers must join the quarriers.
Adding more hands is the quickest way to increase efficiency. We'll also need to get the craftsmen scattered across the mountains together.
If you can carry fifteen tons of marble a day all by yourself...
Then leveling the peak is not impossible.
The rebuilding of the temple was the collective decision of the citizens of Athenius. We certainly won't complain, if we can solve the sunlight problem at the same time.
I'll go talk to the farmers!
Problem solved!
All thanks to you, Titi.
Don't mention it.
It looks amazing, even in its incomplete state... Now, I'm even more curious about the one running this project.
Speaking of which, we still have a problem.
What is it?
The original proponent of the First Temple project, Pallas, went missing last night.
'...Our joy was for both of us, but now eternal peace is yours alone.'
'The breeze in the mountains of Heria and the rivers of the Agnes Plains shall shine in your eyes no more.'
'Neither shall my heart know the tranquility of night and the leisure of day again...'
It's been seven years, Ithakos, old friend.
The tall warrior places the cyclamen flowers before the white grave marker.
His movements are light and gentle, but several white petals still fall upon the ground.
You're late.
I'm simply passing through, on my way to a burdenbeast ranch with a view.
You can find all the excuses you want, but you know you can't let it go.
Neither can I.
You haven't once visited the Endymion Temple in your five years in Athenius.
The first and last time you came was when you brought Ithakos's bloody chiton.
I don't need the guidance of the heroes.
But you continue to dream the same dream. You blame yourself for sending Ithakos across the river, to the battle where he died.
The river runs only in my dreams now, since the Sargonians built a dam upstream.
It brought the melting snow from Heria's peaks to my homeland, and it brought him...
Ithakos was one of the few true friends that I had, one who was never concerned for who I am or what I can do.

His family was tormented by the fires of war, and his father lived most of his life as a broken man. But he would not accept the same fate. His eyes were bright and eager when he received divine revelation.
Whatever the outcome, he found a friend and a brother in you. Did he ever visit you in your dreams, telling you not to mourn him anymore?
It's your place to interpret dreams, and mine to mourn and remember. I will soothe my rage in my own way.
The war is over. Sargon has signed a non-aggression pact with us, and this visit by the Sargonian mission is a symbol of our new relationship.
I know that. Lydia made a point to put me in my place.
The first Astynomos in Athenian history elected by popular vote, empowered to handle diplomatic matters and certain public issues independent of the council and the priests...
She's caused you a fair share of trouble, hasn't she?
Endymion Temple has not been affected yet, but some fringe clerics and the roles of their temples are being marginalized. Besides...
Kereseira's First Temple is nearing completion. There should be a ceremonial play organized by the priests to call the spirit of Kereseira into the temple and grant Minos her protection.
Now, the play is being organized by the Astynomos' Office.
The Astynomos purported to assent when I discussed this matter with her a few days ago, but she was evasive when it came to the program we'd choose...
To her, the priests and their ceremonies are just props to promote Minoan tourism.
Your insistence on using canonized classical drama will only be seen as an act of aggression by the priesthood.
I understand that reform is inevitable, but...
Some things will not and can not change.
Lydia's little power plays on the administrative side are none of my business, but I won't stand idly by if the Sargonian visitors have ill intent.
I'm no hero, just an ordinary Infected, and I don't need the guidance of divine revelation, but I still dream.
If I tell you that I dreamt of a river running red with blood, as Ithakos did nine years ago...
How would you interpret it?
Xanthos!
Excuse me. I have work to do at the crossing, and an apprentice waiting for me.
You're really too kind, Titi. Not only did you solve our dispute with the fruit farmers, you're also helping us look for Pallas.
It's nothing. Besides, I want to meet the one who came up with the temple project, face-to-face.
This Pallas was last seen at the crossing?
Yes.
She was sitting on the docks, drinking and muttering in the direction of distant Mt. Hymnoi.

She's helped us with so many things since the rebuilding of the temple began. Although she doesn't hold any position in the temples of Athenius, and Kassandra from Endymion Temple is the one in charge of the responsibilities that are above-board...
Pallas was the one who took care of everything behind the scenes, from the division of labor to procurement to smoothing things over with the right people.
I see. It's certainly concerning that someone so reliable would go missing all of a sudden...
Ah, well... it's not the first time.
Almost forgot to ask. What does she look like?
A small Forte lady, always seems like she's half-asleep. She wears a little flower on her head.
A little flower... the kind with two leaves, and petals so red you can't see the wrinkles?
Yes! How did you know?
Does it look like that?
The craftsman turns in the direction that Titi is pointing, to see a small red flower swaying gently in a pile of apples.
Yes, it does... but that's one of the apple farmers' crates, isn't it? What's a flower doing there?
Wait, there's something inside! Duck!
It is as though the crate has just woken up, sending ripe apples pouring across the docks, falling into the clear waters of the Delphi Canal.
A Forte slides out from the bed of fruit, rubbing her eyes at the sudden influx of bright sunlight.
P-Pallas?
Guys! Guys! No need for the boat! We found Pallas!
This is Pallas, the people's hero, the proponent and reliable supervisor of the rebuilding plan?
She's not quite what I imagined...
Titi approaches the young woman on the ground curiously.
She examines the horns for signs of battle, trying to determine her weapon's construction, whether it is able to support unique Originium Arts...
She even has the urge to reach for the mysterious little flower, to see if it is a boon from the Minoan heroes.
But Titi senses nothing unusual, except the sweet scent of apples.
The young woman seems to have woken up. Titi leans in to hear what she is muttering.
Mount... Hymnoi...
The western peak... level it... to let the sunlight...
Tell the farmers and workers... what's missing...
Yes... apple cider should be... shared...
Leveling the peak to solve the sunlight problem? So she came to the same conclusion I did... she's thinking about rebuilding the temple, even in her sleep.
Who says a hero has to be big and brawny? How could I forget that I'm in Minos, not Sargon?
Mm... who are you?
We meet at last, Pallas, first proponent of the rebuilding of Kereseira's First Temple.
I'm Medjehtiqedti Nasutu Mashayer, ambassador from Sargon.
Medj...?
My friends call me Titi.
You got the workers and farmers to calm down by having them arm-wrestle as Heraces did? You are a clever one, Titi.
But you already found the solution last night. You just fell asleep in a pile of apples due to exhaustion.
Shame about the apple cider... you were planning to treat the workers and farmers, and get them to work together after they got a little tipsy, weren't you?
Correct. But it's being put to good use to treat an honored guest.
No better medicine for boat sickness, if you're still feeling it! Yia mas!
Yia mas!
Oh, so sweet—this is good!
Please, tell me honestly... even though we had the same idea, was it perhaps inappropriate for me to interfere with the rebuilding of the First Temple?
Because you're the Sargonian ambassador?
Because Sargon was—
Just answer this question, Titi. Do you think the Whitemane King's conquest of Minos was necessary?
No.
Even though it distracted Sargonians from internal strife?
The empire needs to solve its own issues! Blaming it on innocent neighbors is a sign of incompetence.
Although I probably wouldn't be so frank, back in Sargon...
Fine words, fine words! Have another drink! Yamas!
I didn't think a woman so carefree and whimsical would be so pointed when discussing history.
As long as your answer comes from the heart and not concern for your own safety, I think we'll become very good friends.
Priestess Pallas—
Is this how you address a friend?
Then... Just Pallas. I believe one must not forget oneself, no matter where one goes.
Pepe—a friend of mine back in Sargon—says my train of thought is all over the place and I have no common sense, but that's another matter entirely!
Looks like the Sargon padishahs made a wise choice in picking you to lead the mission...
Don't worry, the workers and farmers are very fond of you. We know a bad egg when we see one, and we don't hate all Sargonians.
Besides, at a time like this, simply participating in the rebuilding of the First Temple is hardly crossing any lines, as long as it helps solve problems.
What do you mean?

Look at the pillars, reliefs, and decorations inside the temple. Most of the details are unfinished. The amphitheater outside? There's no slope with the audience seats, so anyone behind the third row can't see what's going on in the center.
And the Kereseira statue that's supposed to be in the middle of the theater? It's still being polished by its perfectionist sculptor.
That's...
A huge-ass problem. No need to weigh your words—it's not like we're writing poetry. Everyone knows that our progress has been way behind schedule.
Have another drink. Opa!
Now, there's no hard-and-fast deadline. Things will get done eventually, even if the pursuit of perfection keeps delaying things. But a lot of people will be affected, if the delays continue.
The orchard harvests...
Yes. Arm-wrestling and cider might not solve things next time... speaking of cider, you're going through it quickly. Want another cup?
Please! I do have a question, though.
What is it?
You help the craftsmen solve their problems, and they rely on you. But you don't look concerned at all, even though you're supposed to be the project's supervisor.
Hm? No, I wasn't ever the supervisor.
Say what now?
The old tradition only calls for a priest, in this case Kassandra, to give the go-ahead. There is no supervisor for the project itself.
It's not a job for an idle drunk like me. However...
You look like a reasonable gal, and you catch on quick.

(Takes another swill) Mm...
What did you say? I was distracted...
No, nothing.
You went through a whole bottle already? You sure can hold your drink. My brew's pretty strong, you know.
Something's not right... didn't you say this cider would help with my boat sickness? I only seem to get dizzier and dizzier...
Hey, booze dizziness beats water dizziness, right? Drink until you pass out, and everything will be fine when you wake up the next morning. Including a bit of boat sickness.
Whuh?
I'm sorry, Father, Mother. I've been infected with Oripathy. Your line of work would never let an Infected take over, so now, I'm free to do what I want, right?
I know I can't stay at home any longer. I'll move out as soon as I can. Don't worry about me, I'm able to care for myself now, and I don't need to trouble my family for anything.
But if it's alright, could you leave my flower garden where it is? They're just plants. Oripathy won't taint them.
Please, let me say goodbye.
It's been a long time since I last came back to Athenius. Did I forget the way?
The public garden west of the agora... I should be in the right place. Where's Kassandra?
Did you think a member of the Athenian Council would meet privately with a Rhodes Islander in a public place?
Looks like you've been away for so long that you've forgotten how things work here.
Lydia? It was you who wanted to see me?
I heard you're the Astynomos of Athenius now? I had wanted to catch up with you sometime, but setting up the Rhodes Island branch office took so much time—
I know. You've done a lot of things for the Infected in the two months that you've been back in Athenius. You could hardly have found the time to come home.
I don't plan on going home, Lydia. They...
Don't worry, you wouldn't run into those vain relatives, even if you went back to the estate.
What is it?
Two years ago, the Athopoulou family declared bankruptcy and quit the herb business. The remaining family members have since moved out of Athenius.
I see...
Looks like you really don't care anymore.
There isn't much worth remembering about the place, other than my garden.
Your mother used to bring you to play with me in the garden. You came to our house after she passed away.
I taught you about plants and their fragrances. You showed little interest, but you planted the blue boronias with me together, just like the ones here...
You've really forgotten.
Wait... don't tell me, this public garden...
This is the flowerbed where you studied and planted medicinal herbs.
Your parents sold the land to the Athenian government to pay off their debts, soon after I became Astynomos.
Did you think they would protect it? Honor the request of the daughter they exiled?
You've changed, Lydia.
So have you.

Visiting the Infected, contacting the healing priests, and even reaching out to Kassandra to make sure that veneration of traditional heroism by the Infected would not get in the way of their treatment.

Handling procedure used to be your great weakness. Now, you've learned to circumvent the parts of city administration where the power structure is yet unclear, and go over the head of the new Astynomos.
I see. You didn't come to reminisce about old times. You want to prevent me from talking to the priests—
To take the authority to establish and maintain the Infected medical system from the Athenian Council and give it to the Astynomos.
You picked up on that fast.
It wouldn't be the first time Rhodes Island has dealt with a power-hungry official.
What Rhodes Island does in an unofficial capacity is none of my business, be it making visits or distributing medicine. You're already doing it, without communication with the Astynomos.
But you know that changes are coming to Minos, be it the rebuilding of the First Temple, the visit of the Sargonian mission, or the establishment of an Oripathy treatment system.
The legends of the Minoan heroes are in our blood, but the same traditions have also brought about convoluted governance, inefficient systems, and backward thinking.
You know that better than anyone else.
I want to say you can do whatever you want, as long as an Oripathy treatment system is established, and my patients get what they need.
But you're the only one in the family I still care about. You're my sister, and I don't want you to put yourself in danger.
Looks like our dear 'ambassador' actually did go strolling off to visit the temple construction site.
All's well, I suppose. She'll be a fine distraction while I dig into this Astynomos' background. Is the ceremony really delayed, or is she intentionally stalling? If she shows us any disrespect...
Make the preparations. We're going to the Astynomos' Office.
What? Who's this girl? Guards!
Beg your pardon, Your Excellency. This is my traveling companion. We hail from Corinia and Lachedamon, invited to attend the ceremony as guests.
She's passionate about Sargonian culture and insisted on catching a glimpse of the ambassador upon hearing that the mission is lodging here. I couldn't stop her.
The ambassador... ahem. You have sharp eyes.
You were invited to attend, though? I haven't heard anything from the Astynomos. I understand that the venue of the exchange ceremony has not been confirmed.
Ah, I shouldn't have babbled, then. Perhaps Lydia intended it to be a surprise.
I am Períandros, priest of Corinia. We learned about the diplomatic mission to Athenius six months ago.
Six months? That long ago?
Yes. The climate of Sargon is very different from that of Minos. Lydia asked me to procure Sargonian-made goods from Corinia in order to make you and the rest of the delegation feel at home.
I hope you are enjoying your accommodations.
Ah, so this was your handiwork.
Lydia has devoted tremendous time and effort to ensuring that the ceremony is a success.
Unfortunately, some of the fabric meant for the costumes for the play have yet to make their way to Hiéreia Kassandra, resulting in a significant delay. Did she mention anything?
A play... the Astynomos did mention that plays are a part of every important occasion.
But who is this Hiéreia Kassandra? I thought this matter was entirely managed by the Astynomos' Office.
Ah, it all makes sense now! I almost misunderstood what she meant. No wonder she left specific instructions not to use the traditional fabric for classical plays. I'm so grateful for the heads-up.

The cotton used for this event is a hybrid of Sargonian and Minoan species. They make a smooth and strong cloth that signifies the lasting peace between our two nations. That's why Lydia insisted on waiting.
I see. So this is the reason she delayed the ceremony...
Delayed? I'm so sorry about that. I really apologize for the trouble. I hope you will not take Lydia's best intentions the wrong way.
Very well. It would be impolite to rush her, if she is taking this ceremony so seriously. Thank you for bringing this to my attention.
You're too kind. Please, make yourselves at home for a few days while preparations for the ceremony continue.
Don't look at me like that. I came with you when you made three rounds of the food stalls in the morning. Only fair that you should come with me here.
How much of what you said was actually true?

The Sargonian delegation has entered the city, and the Astynomos invited us to attend the ceremony, but she didn't tell us anything about her arrangements. What choice do I have but to figure this out on my own?
I assuaged his concerns for her, didn't I?
The merchants of Corinia would not be famous throughout Terra, if profit were the only thing on their minds.
Now, now, it's not polite to say that out loud.
Say ah—
No issues with the respiratory tract or vocal cords. The crystals in the lungs have not spread.
Meaning that when you came to the Rhodes Island office a few days ago and stared blankly at our medics, it wasn't because your condition prevented you from speaking.
Looks like I didn't have to come personally.

You've done a lot for the Infected of Athenius, and Rhodes Island's medicine is far more effective than what we had before. We all appreciate what you've done. I won't take up any more of your time...
A checkup alone isn't enough.

Minoan policies are relatively lenient when it comes to the Infected. With the exception of certain occupations, like the priesthood, the everyday lives of the Infected are usually not significantly affected.
Many people still believe that Oripathy saps the Minoan heroic spirit, though. That's why many patients are indifferent about treatment, even trying to hide their conditions.
How many fingers am I holding up?
Four.
Thank you for the concern, Kyría Athopoulou, but my condition is mild. The illness has not affected my vision.
Then why did you visit the Rhodes Island office four times in the past two weeks?
Well...
You're no great liar. Besides, I saw a Rhodes Island monitoring bracelet on Xanthos, your mentor.
Have you been obtaining medication on his behalf?
Yes...
He forgets his appointments because he has so many things on his plate. It's only reasonable that I should pick up his medicine for him.
But you did not register his name with the office, which means there are no records of him having contracted Oripathy.
He saved my life, taught me the lore of the water and the art of combat. He's been a good mentor to me. I didn't want people to know...
To know that he couldn't be a hero?
Visiting the Oripathy clinic may color how others view you and Xanthos, but it will not change the path that you choose for yourselves.
I'm not Kassandra, and soothing the heart isn't my thing. But I know that both of you need proper treatment.
I'll move the two of you up the priority list and follow up closely on your conditions. You can prepare yourselves for a LOT of nagging.
You're more tenacious than you look.
I wouldn't have to be, if I could buy your cooperation with a few grains of corn like the fowlbeast perching on your bow.
Sorry...
Where is Xanthos, anyway?
If your recent reports were indeed his, then he's suffered significant fluctuations in internal Originium levels during this time. He needs rest, as I said.
I told him, but...
Don't tell me he went to the crossing again?
I'm busy preparing boats for the fruit farmers and craftsmen. Make it quick, Hekádemos.
...Why isn't Lykeion around to help? I never thought he would let you out of his sight.
He took a day off. Home visit by a Rhodes Island doctor.
Home visit?
(Did the medicine he picked up not work? Is he so sick he can't even walk?)
If you came to communicate the Astynomos' 'suggestion', you're wasting your time. Tell her I'll deal with the problem at the crossing, and there's no need for her to worry.
Actually, she sent me to inform you that the problem has been resolved.
Resolved? Pallas acted quickly this time.
No, it wasn't her who came up with the solution.
Who was it, then? Did Kassandra calm the people down, or did the Astynomos pull another one of her little tricks?
Neither.
It was the Sargonian ambassador who resolved the dispute between the craftsmen and the fruit farmers. A small Feline with dark skin and black ears.
A black-eared Feline...
That 'Columbian' I ferried at the secondary crossing that morning...
She was the Sargonian ambassador?
Ancient Minoans saw the beauty of marble, and sculpted legends and epics with hammers and chisels.
In the Kerespereia, Kereseira walked through war and plague, wearing out thirty pairs of shoes, before she arrived at the mountain-rimmed garden.
At the site of the First Temple, she saw a boulder split, and received a revelation from the gods.
Her gentle and wise visage reflected the Athenian morning sun; she protected the refugees who followed her and drove the outlanders out with her sword; and her harp—
'Sings only the tales of the heroes!'
You're familiar with the Kerespereia?
I love it! I've read it so many times I could practically recite it from memory. Kereseira gave me my first impression of the heroes of Minos.
'A hero must be a paragon of justice and kindness—'
'A hero must face fate bravely when it comes, and carry their own burden.'
You weren't exaggerating when you said you could recite it.
But reading words printed on a page doesn't compare to the experience of standing in front of Kereseira's statue.
Even if the sculptor exaggerated Kereseira's miracles, even if those were not her original Arts—
Perhaps it's this artistic license that makes it feel so alive. I can easily picture it standing on this fertile soil a century or ten later...
Are you thinking you'd be willing to wait a decade or more for Kereseira's First Temple to be completed to the standard of this statue?
Er, not quite. I want to see as much as I can before the exchange is done and I have to leave Minos.
So, Kereseira's statue is complete, and the amphitheater has proper steps now?
Thanks in large part to your suggestion. The fruit farmers have been working even harder than the craftsmen in the quarries, in order to get sunlight to the orchards as soon as possible.
Stone meant for the temple is being used on the amphitheater first? I read about it in the books, but the Minoan love of theater is really something.
Heroes come from the people, and the people aspire to become heroes, even if only in drama.
Have you identified the inspiration for this statue?
Kereseira and the Outlander, Chapter 11 of the Kerespereia. It was adapted into a famous classical drama that is often used in temple ceremonies honoring the heroes.
But I've visited a few theaters in my time here, and I haven't seen it on the program anywhere.
Are plays meant for religious ceremonies banned in normal theaters?
No, Kereseira and the Outlander is part of the standard classical repertoire. It has not been staged for a century, however, even in the temples.
A century... could it be...?
Yes, it was banned during the Sargonian occupation.
The play's setting dates back to before the establishment of Minos as a nation, and 'outlanders' refers to those not of the ancient Agamem Kingdom in general.
When the Sargonian padishahs banned the play, the outlander executed by Kereseira took on a certain meaning.
The ban is no more, but to play the outlander in the play means playing a Sargonian, and no Athenian would be willing to do that.
What a shame...
Perhaps. To the Minoans, plays are more than just entertainment and art.
Be it the ban on performances, or the burning of the theaters, the heroism inherent in the history and legend depicted by the plays is not so easily forgotten.
Even if no one performs it, and the music accompanying the play can no longer be heard on the streets, any Athenian can still recite its most famous scenes. That's enough.
No...
What is it?
The First Temple was destroyed by war, and Kereseira and the Outlander was banned... there is no denying that Sargon has brought great pain to Minos.
The padishahs' order to return the Minoan relic is not so much compensation as political posturing...
My role as the Sargonian ambassador is just to escort the relic to its destination. I'm not from an influential family, nor do I have a distinguished career. There isn't much I can do.
But I'm still the curator of the Varjavandabad Museum. Knowledge will not betray me.
The relic I am bringing is associated with Kereseira, and has great significance to Minos... this should not be a mere political bargaining chip.
Titi looks up at the statue of Kereseira with her harp, beautiful and awe-inspiring.
She seems to hear the harp sing a song that only the heroes are meant to hear, and see the familiar stories and legends flash before her eyes.
The amphitheater is barely finished. The scaffolds and tarps have yet to be taken away. In them, however, Titi feels something special—
Something that belongs only to Minos, written in the epics, carved into marble by the stonemasons, something that has endured and still stands tall, even after a century of oppression...
I want to hold the exchange ceremony here.
Really? That's an interesting idea, but...
But matters concerning Kereseira's First Temple must be discussed with the temple priests.
Kassandra? What brings you here today?
Forgive me for interrupting. There is something that I must discuss with Pallas in private, Your Excellency.
This is the first time you've shown, since that young acolyte of yours glared at me last week.
It's not easy, managing Endymion Temple and the rebuilding of the First Temple at the same time.
Folia is just worried that I'm overworked. She knows we've been friends since our priest school days.
Which made it all the harder to accept that I brought you trouble as soon as I got back, I guess.
Pallas...
I knew we were both in trouble when you showed up at Endymion Temple to have your dream interpreted as an ordinary Minoan instead of using the priests' passage, six months ago.
In most cases, divine revelation can be classified into certain common categories, whether it came in the form of a dream or a sudden vision.
I know. We learned them by heart at school, so ordinary priests without special talents could guide those who received revelations.
But the vision that you brought was not found in any classic tale... I had no doubt that it was a powerful revelation, which made me very uneasy.
Don't worry. We'll find a way.
You shouldn't have quit the priesthood. You might have been able to find help...
And hide my condition, continuing to be the legendary 'Goddess of Victory' on the border?
I didn't want to complicate things, even if another has taken up the title. Besides, the heroes came a long way to call me back. I had to be honest with myself.
...Looks like Rhodes Island has treated you well.
Of course. Did I ever tell you that I founded a 'Minoan League' on Rhodes Island?
Are there so many Minoans who left home to seek treatment?
There are plenty, although Erato and Vulcan are the only true Minoans in the league. The others are in it because they have a spiritual fascination with our country.
I'll introduce them, when we have a chance. They're all full of heroic spirit, with great passion for poetry, art, and brewing.
So you founded a drinking league.
That's one way to put it. It's not quite the same as having you around, though. You may not hold your drink particularly well, but no one in our year brewed better than you.
I remember how you tied up your sleeves and pounded the grapes like a seasoned winemaker at the intercollegiate conference. You looked amazing.
I see you have a craving. What happened to the formula I gave you?
I lost it when I went to Akroti. I finally escaped my torment when my Sarkaz bodyguard gave me the recipe for the secret brew of Kazdel.
I never worried you would have trouble making friends. First the Sarkaz bodyguard, now the Sargonian ambassador.
You heard about the fruit farmers and the craftsmen. Titi can be trusted.
I don't doubt Kyría Mashayer's cleverness, or her friendly outlook towards our country, but her position remains sensitive.
If I didn't stop you, would you have put her in charge of the play that we already discussed, in addition to the venue of the exchange ceremony?
Is it that you trust her too much, or...?
You're frowning again, Kassandra. I just let myself get a little lazy, having been away from the priesthood for so long.
You would never have become a legend in the borderlands, if you were the type to shirk responsibility.
I don't mind taking charge of the rebuilding project on your behalf. I just think it's a shame. You're better at dealing with people, and the craftsmen trust you.
I thought you made the proposal to the people so they would return to heroic tradition and remember the heroic spirit.
I did.
And that's exactly why it doesn't matter who's in charge.
Our people venerate the heroes, but they've gradually forgotten that Minoan prosperity was not only built by a few heroes.
'The heroes came from the people, from all walks of life.'
You could always read my mind.
I know what you're trying to say... but with the rebuilding of the temple entering its final stage, it's even more important than ever to have a supervisor.
I think we'll have one soon.
What is it?
I saw Lydia headed to the temple square.
What do you think this Astynomos, who has quietly been at odds with us from the beginning and is trying to take the play out of the priests' hands, is going there for?
We meet at last, Your Excellency Ambassador Mashayer.
And who might you be?
I'm the Astynomos of Athenius, called Lydia. I am overseeing this relic exchange between our two nations.
Oh! I'm so sorry, Astynomos. I should have visited you first thing when I got to Athenius. I didn't think...
You came into Athenius by the ancient route to follow in Kereseira's footsteps, didn't you?
There is no need to apologize to a Minoan for your love of our epics, our culture.
If anything, I should be thanking you for resolving the dispute between the fruit farmers and craftsmen. The rebuilding of the First Temple could not have proceeded so smoothly without you.
I simply did my part.
I believe you've already met Behnui? I take it the venue and details of the exchange ceremony have not been confirmed.
Correct.
Rather than do it behind closed doors in a private room, what do you think of holding the ceremony beneath the statue of Kereseira, to be witnessed by her and all of Athenius?
You want to hold the exchange ceremony at the First Temple square?

Yes. You are aware that I brought the most precious treasure of the Varjavandabad Museum, to be exchanged for the technical data that the Sargonians left behind during the construction of the Three Poleis.
It's a part of many heroic Minoan stories, and closely linked to Kereseira herself.
Seeing the completed statue on the square was an awe-inspiring experience. Her brave visage and the relic that I carry complement each other.
I understand that the exchange ceremony is supposed to begin with a classical play, in accordance with Minoan tradition. What better place to do it than the First Temple's new amphitheater?
Would that be a problem?
Oh, Kassandra from Endymion Temple reminded me that any use of the First Temple should be discussed with her...
Kassandra...
It's the Astynomos' Office's responsibility to lighten the burden on the busy priests by handling trivial matters. I'll communicate with her.

I was concerned about the lack of proper supervision in the rebuilding process because of the emulation of ancient methods, which has resulted in significant delays. I thought it might not be the most dignified of venues.
I don't mind—
However, all our problems could be solved if you are officially made the supervisor of the rebuilding project.
Say what now?
The rebuilding of the temple is entering its final stages. We've reached the point where decisiveness and efficiency are more important than careful planning.
I'm sure you already have a fair idea of what is going on, having befriended the craftsmen and solved their issues.
Besides, I understand that you received an advanced education in museology in Columbia, including studies in architecture.
Putting the finishing touches on the temple and readying it for opening shouldn't present too much difficulty, should it?
I mean, it's true that I designed and supervised the expansion of my museum, but it's not nearly as big as this temple...
Besides, is it really appropriate for a Sargonian ambassador to supervise the rebuilding of the First Temple?
Your request that the exchange ceremony take place at the First Temple is ample proof of your understanding of the relic and Minoan culture.
I am in full support of your proposal, and I'm sure the ever-considerate Kassandra won't object.
But...
I didn't expect the ambassador to be such a polite and humble young lady.
Períandros?
Greetings from Corinia, land of trade and merchants, Your Excellency Ambassador Mashayer of Sargon.
I am here as Corinia's representative to the exchange ceremony, at the Astynomos' request. I happened to overhear your conversation and couldn't help but stop to listen.
May I ask you a question—would you participate in the rebuilding of the First Temple, if your status were not a concern?
Of course!

It would be a great honor to participate in the restoration of such a wonderful example of Minoan architecture, not to mention the knowledge that could be gained! I would be delighted just to hew one piece of marble for it!
In that case, why trouble yourself unnecessarily?
Trouble myself... what?
I have a little suggestion that might solve our present problem. As I see it, the two of you are in full agreement, but for this little issue of Ambassador Mashayer's status.

It's a sensitive issue, and Ambassador Mashayer's concerns are certainly well-founded. On the other hand, the Astynomos here is seeking a capable supervisor who has the support of the people, in order to keep the temple project on track.
What better way to resolve this than Minos's most ancient process?
You mean...?
A vote.
A referendum held on the First Temple square. Let the citizens of Athenius participating in the rebuilding decide whether to accept Ambassador Mashayer's supervision or not.
...She's really the Sargonian ambassador.
Travesty! The theater of Kereseira's First Temple was not built to host a diplomatic farce!
That Sargonian said she brought a relic in exchange for the technical data from the Three Poleis? I knew that cargo didn't feel like movie figurines.
The shape, the weight, and its relevance to Kereseira... could it be...?
The gall! To use stolen Minoan treasure to buy Minoan trust!
Not if I have anything to say about it...
Great Kereseira, gentle and brave, not even the night can hide your splendor.
You brought us a wise duo to solve our troubles and help us build your temple.
The insects sing the song of your harp, your laurel is woven into the shape of victory, and the moonlight—
Oh, sorry, it's not moonlight that shines upon you, but the spotlights that we installed last week.
(Hearty drinking)
Don't drink too much. We're here to vote, remember? You don't want to hurt your hand engraving your ostracon.
Of course I remember! I'm here to vote for Titi!
I almost fell when I was working on the statues by myself in the temple. I wouldn't be drinking here right now if Titi didn't save me with her Arts!
Uh...

You don't believe me?
Let me show you. I tripped over the statue's sash that trails on the floor, and then—
Whoa!
Look out!
Several white ribbons fly out of the shadows towards the drunken Forte girl, helping her steady herself.
They let her go once they ascertain that she is unharmed, straightening her hair while patting her horns in reproach.
See? Those ribbons saved my ass, just like that.
Oh, Titi! What are you doing here? Let's get a drink!
Pallas told me about a girl in the moving crew who's small but strong. She loves drinking and has a tendency to stumble.
But I didn't expect to see you stumble a dozen times in just three days, Kantia.
Thanks, Titi.
She doesn't hold her drink any better than the average Minoan, which is to say she's supposed to drink in moderation. But she has a drunkard's soul.
Hehe...
You need a swig, Titi?
Do I look that nervous?
Not on your face, but we've all figured out that your tail doesn't lie.
Mm...
Who's the guy under the scaffolds, wearing a leaf-shaped ring on his grey tail?
Serafim from the paint crew. He's only motivated when working with big, muscular dudes. Nobody believes him when he says he's just lazy.
What about the Elafia sitting in the corner, not talking to anyone?
Lefteria, from the sculpting crew. She always stays out of the sun. I've only talked to her a few times, but I know she's obsessive about her work.
That's more than most of us know about her. Most of the craftsmen barely know she exists.
I don't know about the others, Titi, but to me, you're one of us.
Thanks, Manos.
Shouldn't you be comforting your friend? She looks nervous.
Comforting people is your job, Kassandra of Endymion Temple.
What Titi needs now is not the reassurance of a friend, but the support of the Minoans participating in the temple project.
Kyría Mashayer has been reciting her speech silently before those two craftsmen struck up a conversation with her.
She subconsciously clutches her tail whenever she trips over a word. Twice she accidentally grabbed her little pet instead, much to its annoyance.
You've been observing her carefully. I think you like her.
This alone is not enough to prove that she's worthy of trust. I just didn't want to surrender my own judgment to prejudice.
It didn't take you long to decide that she was the best choice to supervise the project. I trust your judgment.
Tonight, there is no shadow of confusion in those reflected in Kereseira's eyes. Everyone has made their decisions. They do not need the words of the heroes to guide their way.
I have some personal matters to attend to. Excuse me.
You're going?
The Astynomos might take the absence of the supervising priest from the voting as a form of protest.
My ostracon is already in the box. Besides, Lydia has no need for such blatant excuses, if she wants to pick a bone with the priesthood.
If the exchange ceremony takes place at the First Temple, then the ceremonial play to honor the heroes...
I'll talk to Lydia again, at least get her to defer to the priests when it comes to choosing the play.
But that's all for another day. Let's hope tonight's vote goes smoothly.
—Based on my experiences these past few days, once we solved the issue with the stone, I believe the structure is essentially complete and ready to welcome visitors.
It will be ready to open in a few days once we put on the finishing touches and complete the inspections according to plan.
There wouldn't be too much work to do to set up the exchange ceremony on the square. We'd just need to clear some room for the ceremony itself, and have a separate area for logistics.
We don't need to change anything with the rest of the building; it'll be ready to open once construction is complete.
I will create an actionable proposal based on my knowledge and experience overseeing construction projects.
If I'm elected, we will work together to complete the wonder that is Kereseira's First Temple!
Such a lively sight! It seems this Kyría Mashayer has made a lot of friends in a short time.
It would undoubtedly be a massive boon to the friendship between our two countries, not to mention the temple project, if she's elected.
What do you want?
I'm not sure what you mean. I'm here to attend the ceremony, at your invitation.
A son of Corinia's merchants just happens to pass by as I am talking to the Sargonian ambassador, and casually drops a solution to the problem at hand that leads to tonight's vote.
Was this all Kereseira's will?
Why not?
Tradition dictates that any Minoan who has received the heroes' blessing must visit a temple the following day to declare it before a priest.
But you will not seek Hiéreia Kassandra no matter what, no matter how close the Astynomos' Office is to Endymion Temple, will you?
You're crossing the line, Hiereús Períandros.
You know as well as I do that the election of Kyría Mashayer will be more than just a feather in your diplomat's cap. Your foremost concern will always be Minos itself, as it is for me.
Speak your demands. You can spin all the words you want, but I will not assent before seeing the terms and conditions of the deal.
Have patience, Honored Astynomos.
Reputation is the most important thing in business. And I will bring my business to you personally, once the dust has settled.
It's not that time yet.
In accordance with tradition, please etch 'Yea' or 'Nay' onto the ostracon you are holding.
Then, place it in the box face-down.
We will tally the results once everyone has cast their ostraca.
If more than half the votes are in favor, Titi... Kyría Mashayer shall be named supervisor of the temple project, until the exchange ceremony is completed.
Begin.

lines.st: The crowd walks up, one by one, to place their ostraca.
There is some distance between the box and the center of the square where Titi is standing, but she can see the smiling faces of the friends she has made these past few days.
They nod to Titi as their gazes meet.
She cannot see the words on their ostraca, but she knows what vote they cast.
Who would have thought that she would be in charge of the construction of a Minoan temple? It would have been a tremendous honor, even in Padishah Malla's time.
How should she repay the kindness the Athenians have shown her?

lines.x: Hah. What a joke.
A voice interrupts Titi's thoughts, and causes the square to fall silent.
Is this the year 1100?
I thought maybe we were at the turn of the millennium, and the Sargonians had come back to rule us.
The war is over, Megas.
For you, maybe. Not for me.
The hated Sargonians continue to hound me in my darkest dreams.
I wish I were as forgetful as you. But our heroes tell me in my dreams that I must not forget.
Did a priest interpret that for you?
No. But these recurring dreams are the heroes' voice. I alone know their will.
I will never smile and work alongside a Sargonian.
I will not stand for it, no matter what good things you may say the Sargonian has done.
Titi watches as the Minoan passes in front of her without so much as casting a glance her way.
He tosses his ostracon as he passes by the box. It hits the side and falls onto the ground, rolling a few times before settling face-up. The ostracon is blank.
Titi realizes that his hatred of Sargonians is such that he would not dignify this vote by participating.
Her cheeks burn as though she has been slapped in the face, but she trembles as she feels a chill run up her spine.
Everything seems to grow distant. It is as though there is a thick screen separating her and the others, and all the sounds around her.
In her daze, she seems to perceive Pallas stepping up, and the craftsmen casting concerned glances at her.
She wants to say that she is fine, she is alright, that she was prepared for such treatment, as an envoy of peace sent by the erstwhile invaders and oppressors.
Besides, it's not as though the Minoan has done anything to her. He simply said he did not want to work with her. This is fine; no one can be loved by everyone, after all.
She wants to smile, that famous bright, carefree smile of Medjehtiqedti Nasutu Mashayer's, which has charmed so many visitors to her museum.
But she cannot find the words, or her smile.
The words of the Minoan who tossed the ostracon echo in her mind. She recalls the ferryman who questioned her the day she arrived in Athenius.
The time she spent with the temple craftsmen meant something. They still trust her.
But to the countless Minoan strangers out there, she is not Titi, the curious lover of Minoan legend. She is a Sargonian.
Everyone's gone, and the lights are out. How long are you planning to keep yourself curled up here? That Forte girl and the craftsmen were worried sick about you.
What are you gonna do, tell them to go on holiday while you fix the temple overnight with your hands and ribbons? That'll make things square, will it?
I've been circling your feet and winking at you for ages. It never occurred to you to look down? Never noticed that something wasn't quite right?

Did you notice that a-hole deputy excusing himself from the voting ceremony and distancing himself from the whole thing? Did you notice the words 'ulterior motives' written all over that Astynomos? No, you just couldn't resist jumping into the grave that's been dug just for you.
Now look where it's gotten you.

Really? You're crying over that? I can't even tell you how many times I've seen it in the City of Gold, where licking boots and stamping on faces is as much a part of everyday life as eating and shitting.
Besides, I doubt you're crying for the people who used you.
I told you when you got the padishah's assignment that it wasn't gonna be a pleasant vacation.

The scars of history that run deep, the Minoans who are still recovering from the pain, and your position as the ambassador of Sargon, all serve to remind you that it is not just an exchange of relics that you have to deal with.
The proud creature tries to catch a glimpse of the young Feline's face, in a rare show of concern, but her head is bowed too low for it to see.
