Mio sighs and pokes Titi's hand with its head, cuddling around her and gestures for her to pet it.
Furry makes one happy. Your words.
I give you special permission to stroke my tail, fluff the hair on my back, or wipe your tears on either.
Actually, I take back the last one. I hate getting wet.
Why did I even pick a trouble-making little thing like you? Now I'm on consolation duty.
Heh...
If I am really so fragile, you wouldn't have picked me when you left the Grand Bazaar, would you?
Just this one time to lift your mood. Don't you dare get carried away.
Thanks, Mio.
Feeling better?
Yes. Some people voted for me. It means they trust me and have hopes for me.
Manos called me one of them.
People don't always get along, even in the same family. And I'm in a country that was deeply hurt by Sargon in the past.
I'm not a hero of legend. I can't miraculously solve everything with a song, a poem, or a speech.
What I can do is to get Kereseira's First Temple completed as soon as possible, and return to the Minoans, to Kereseira, the things that belong to Minos.
Moonlight shines upon Kereseira's complexion. Wearing a laurel wreath with her hand on her harp, she gazes into the infinite distance.
Titi stands beneath the marble statue, bathing in the same silent moonlight as Kereseira. She closes her eyes and makes a wish.
'And so Kereseira, the singer of epics, the gentlest hero of Agamem, hung her harp upon the branches of the laurel tree—'
'Its body made from the tallest firs atop Mount Hymnoi, its strings made from the bravest burdenbeast that roamed the Agnes Plains;'
'When it played, all things in the Land of Heroes sang together, riding upon the winds in freedom.'
No wonder you came up with the idea of holding the exchange ceremony on the temple square when you saw the completed statue of Kereseira.
You brought Kereseira's true harp, the one that used to be displayed in the First Temple of Old Athenius?
Yes!
Legend has it that Kereseira was inspired to carve a massive harp from mountain rocks and firs after hearing the mountains tell their stories beneath Mt. Hymnoi.
It was a blessed harp that only sang on important occasions, or when played by a great hero like Kereseira, offering a song that touches the people's hearts.
The First Temple was destroyed when the Sargonians invaded Old Athenius.
Grieving the tragedy, the harp sang no more, and afterwards went missing.
Until Padishah Khery found it in his collection.
I don't know how it ended up in Sargon—perhaps a Sargonian soldier looted it from Old Athenius and sold it to the padishah's ancestor.
I don't think anyone alive in Athenius has ever seen the original harp.
The only people who would know anything about the harp are a few priests well-versed with ancient texts, or oribenders like Kassandra who directly communicate with the heroes.
Does it still make a sound?
Yes.
We performed an inspection on the strings. They're a little aged, but still in playing condition.
I see. Perhaps the silence of the harp was simply a metaphor, as is often the case in Minoan stories...
Where are the ferrymen?
We pay these outrageous rents to be by the canal and get fresh fins!
The goods are here, keeper.
Finally! Any more delays, and we won't be ready for the diplomatic delegation!
Should I unload the goods for you?
Hey, why is the boat docked so far today? People will be falling into the water trying to unload stuff.
Bring the boat closer, please. As close to the kitchen as you can.
Okay.
Thank you. Why are you the one delivering today, by the way? It's usually Xanthos who does it.
I apologize. Master Xanthos had business.
Well, it's no problem. As long as the food gets here and the kitchen can get to work.
You've been with Xanthos for a few years, haven't you? He must have his reasons for planning it this way. Not concerned at all.
Sorry, Pallas. Everyone in the temple has their hands full today. I simply can't free up anyone from the moving crew.
I wanted to get the harp into the First Temple ahead of time to see if how it fits Kereseira's statue.
I should have had someone big and strong to help with carrying the harp, though. It's just the two of us...
Kereseira was not particularly tall according to historical records. People took particular note of the contrast when she stood next to her harp.
Maybe I could even do it myself—
Oof... it's heavy. Is this box full of lead or something?
Well, we entered Athenius by the ancient route, which is pretty rough. We couldn't risk any damage to the relic.
I had the museum staff prepare a packaging that's fireproof, waterproof, shockproof, and impact-proof, which unfortunately added a ton of weight to it.
On the other hand, it wouldn't get a scratch on it, even if an earthquake suddenly shook a whole house down onto it!
I can see you take great care of your museum and collection.
I went to Columbia to study museology because I was curious about everything, and ended up working in a museum when I went back to Sargon.
But my father died young, and my family's a far cry from its past glory... honestly, I'm not sure why the padishah picked me to lead the diplomatic mission...
But it's my job now, and I want to do it right!
Did I ever tell you that you—
What happened?! Everything okay, Titi?
Don't worry, the harp's unharmed!
I was asking about YOU!
Speaking of earthquakes... good thing the relic is well-protected.
What's all the commotion downstairs?
Looks like a boat rammed straight into the hotel kitchen.
It's over... it's all over...
What kind of a ferryman are you?! Full sails from five meters away? On a windy day like this? You knew your boat would go even faster than Hermes the Messenger!
You wanted the boat as close to the kitchen as possible.
I didn't mean INSIDE the kitchen!
Make way, make way.
Peace officer's already dispersing onlookers. Don't crowd around like this. I need to see what happened.
Lykeion?
You'll uphold justice, won't you, Grammateús?
I just wanted the boat bringing the ingredients to be closer to the kitchen, but this kid rammed it right into the place!
I'm sorry. I... don't know how to operate a boat.
Who do you think you're fooling? You've been studying with Xanthos at the crossing every day for years!
Theoretical knowledge does not equal practical skill.
Why you—!
Enough!
I can testify that this man never passed a single chariot racing test back in priest school.
Doesn't explain how he didn't see this huge stop sign, does it? The boat ran right through the fence!
Didn't see...?
(Has Oripathy spread to his perceptual system? Is that why Lena made a home visit the other day?)
Lykeion...
Why do you look like you think I'm going to die soon—
No, no, no! Don't say that! Even if it's true...
Excuse me, Grammateús. I know the two of you used to be classmates, but you're here as an official, and my kitchen has suffered significant damage.
Here.
This is... your coin purse?
I'll pay for the damage. Take what you need.
Who's telling you to pay for me?
You never got a proper job since you left priest school. I know Xanthos takes care of your room and board, but I doubt you have much money saved.
Don't worry about it. Spend your money on treatment. Don't forget you still owe me a debate.
I'm fine, really... *cough* *cough*
The coughing... an acute attack? Doctor! I need a doctor over here!
It's just the smoke, you idiot! Go get someone to fight the fire, Grammateús!
The fire is getting stronger. Looks like the boat hit the kitchen stove... *cough* What's with all the smoke?
We need to get out, get away from the smoke. But the harp...
The harp's well-protected. I see the officers are already organizing people to fight the fire. It should be under control soon.
Besides, the stairs are too narrow. The harp would get in the way of other people evacuating. Besides... *cough*—
Are you alright, Pallas?
You're right. We need to leave. We can come back when the smoke's gone. Let's go.
A fire? Oh no, oh no! My luggage is still in my room!
Don't worry, the fire's in the first-floor kitchen. It's not going to spread to the guestrooms upstairs. Just wait for them to put out the fire and the smoke to disperse.
Stop crowding and clear the area around the door! There are still people coming down from upstairs—
Sir! Now's not the time to go inside!
Feeling better, Pallas?
I'm fine. I just inhaled some smoke.
We should be good to go back to the room in ten minutes. I'll help you take the harp to the First Temple, just in case anything else happens.
Thanks.
Oh, sorry. It's so crowded here.
Hey, Pallas, did that guy look familiar to you?
Who?
Did you hear? That kid at the crossing was the one who rammed the boat into the kitchen. He usually looks pretty smart, but I guess he's as dumb as it gets when it comes to piloting a boat.
Where was Xanthos? He's the one who usually delivers the goods.
Too busy, maybe? But it's not like him to let an inexperienced apprentice get into trouble on his own.
True.
...I remember now. It was Xanthos, the ferryman, who ran into me just now.
Xanthos? Are you sure?
If he had the time to come to the hotel, why would he let an inexperienced apprentice steer the boat and cause an accident? Wait a second...
I have a bad feeling about this.
As do I.
The room of the Sargonian ambassador... this is the relic that was stolen a century ago?
The tall Minoan peers at the massive wooden box as he starts the motor on his serrated oar, repurposing the tool that he uses to cross the river to pick the lock open.
He carefully lifts the cover of the box. The instrument of Kereseira, which should have come home long ago, is safely packaged in a dust cover.
Kereseira's harp must not be used as a bargaining chip by the Sargonian hypocrites, to be shut away in this pretentious, suffocating box...
Put the harp down, Xanthos.
The people praise you for proposing the rebuilding of the First Temple as soon as you returned to Athenius, Pallas. I voted in favor myself.
Thank you.
But you should not have cozied up to the Sargonians.
You instructed your apprentice to ram the hotel kitchen and cause a fire, letting you sneak in to steal an artifact of great diplomatic significance to our two countries... it's an ill-advised act.
The streets outside the hotel are packed with people. You can't possibly escape with the harp.
You're jumping out the window? This is the fourth floor!
We're directly above the kitchen. The boat that rammed the kitchen wasn't just a distraction, it's his escape... he had this planned.
The door will take too much time. How about—
You give me a ride?
Wisdom.
The fire is under control, Grammateús. As for the hotel's losses...
I'll report the incident to the Astynomos' Office per official procedures. As for the damage to the kitchen, I've already settled up with the innkeeper.
I see. We'll station someone here to make sure no one's trying to take advantage of the chaos, for instance...
What is it?
L-Look up!
Hekádemos looks up.

A pair of Forte horns, pitch-black under the sunlight like the twin moons, flies over the head of the crowd, their owner landing upon a haystack on the accident boat, before leaping off the boat and fleeing along the river.
A few seconds later, a white figure leaps from the window off the end of a silver chain. The black Feline's landing is softened by several cloth ribbons surrounding her.
Xanthos the ferryman? And the Sargonian ambassador?
What's that huge thing Xanthos is carrying on his back...? Don't tell me...
You were right, officer. Someone IS trying to take advantage of the chaos.
Understood. Cordon the scene and impound that boat! Send someone to follow Kyría Mashayer and bring Xanthos into custody!
The harvest is done. We'll need your help again packing the apples in crates.
No problem. You guys helped a lot with the stone for the temple, after all.
Excuse me.
Whoa, whoa—
Xanthos? What's the hurry?
Is that Titi chasing after him?
Hey, Thanos! M-Make way!
Is this a race? Where's the finish line?
Go, Titi, go! You can't lose to Xanthos when he has so much stuff on his back, right?
No I can't!
Careful with the apples!
The Sargonian is persistent...
But this is as far as you go.
Xanthos! The river is just ahead!
The harp is out of its waterproof packaging! The water will ruin it!
I don't need a Sargonian to remind me, not when your people have ruined countless Minoan treasures.
Xanthos!
Wait, what's that sound... there's a boat under the pier? He had this carefully planned. It wasn't an impulsive act.
No, don't go—
Arts-controlled ribbons fly toward the package on Xanthos's shoulder.
The Forte instinctively jumps back, placing the harp at the stern of the ship. The ribbons wind themselves around his arm, helping Titi jump onto the boat.
Xanthos! Ugh... it's swaying beneath my feet...
We need to clear the air between us. Let's talk about this on dry land, shall we?
I have nothing to say to a Sargonian.
Let me apologize, then...
The two keep their eyes fixed on each other, one at the bow, the other at the stern. Xanthos clutches his oar and starts its motor, while Titi begins to glow with the light of her Originium Arts.
Ribbons fly from her clothes, grabbing the harp and pulling it towards the bow.
Xanthos cuts the ribbons, only to find that Titi has closed the distance with the harp. He runs to the bow, causing the boat to tip violently, throwing Titi off-balance and interrupting her Arts.
Both of them reach out desperately—
The dust cover on the relic is removed, revealing the legendary lost harp, in all its glory.
Every Minoan has heard the story of Kereseira and her harp, but precious few have seen it with their own eyes.
It remains proud and glorious, despite the passage of a thousand years. Sunlight falls on the metallic leaves decorating its pedestal, surrounding it with a gentle glow.
The pair fighting over the harp has no time to admire, as they watch the harp's trajectory change, less than a second before it hits the ground.
Medjehtiqedti has never thought of herself as lucky.

The glory of her noble house had long faded away by her father's generation. Her father was obsessed with literature, while her mother, still clinging to old aristocratic habits, showed little interest in spending time with her daughter.
Titi spent most of her free time in her father's library, alone.

The knowledge she learned there was basic, shallow, but it nevertheless expanded her horizons, and instilled in her a strong sense of curiosity for everything. That was why she saved money to pay for her own studies in museology in Columbia.
She was excited, but nervous, when Padishah Khery appointed her to be the ambassador to Sargon.
On one hand, she was drawn to the wonders of Minoan culture and the stories of heroes; on the other hand, she was acutely aware of the challenges that the burden of history presented.
The joy that she gained from working with the temple craftsmen, the last visage of fantasy and daydream in her mind, would be shattered with the falling harp in less than a second.
Xanthos was not so arrogant as to think that his plan to take the harp back from the Sargonians was flawless.
He might go to jail, or have an Oripathy attack while carrying out the plan. He did not care that he was risking his life on something that would seem ridiculous to most.
To him, the only place where he would find peace was the battle on the border where Ithakos went, five years ago. There was no point in asking the meaning of anything that has happened since.
But the trickery of fate is even more cruel than in the heroic legends. Xanthos thought of many ends for himself, but never this one.
The precious harp is about to hit the ground, and there is nothing Xanthos can do about it. Should he be angry? Sad? Fearful? Or simply laugh at his own helplessness?
The emotions that fate has impounded have returned to his empty chest. The tall Minoan warrior sees everything flash before his eyes.
The breeze of the Delphi Canal brushes against Xanthos's back. It has never felt so cold. He cannot help but tremble.
He can see the Sargonian woman's face. It is deathly pale. He knows that his own face is the same.
We've arrested Xanthos the ferryman and his apprentice Lykeion on charges of robbery, Your Excellency.
I sincerely apologize for the damages you've suffered... I know it's not my place to ask, but can the damaged item be—
Thank you for your concern, officer. The item that was damaged was a personal effect, and not particularly valuable.
It was Xanthos the ferryman who ferried me across the river when I first came to Athenius. This was simply a minor misunderstanding. You can send the onlookers on their way.
Yes, Your Excellency.
If you do not intend to pursue this matter any further, then we will take the two suspects and proceed in accordance with the law.
You two! Thank Kyría Mashayer for her magnanimity.
You don't know how to steer a boat. Grammateús Hekádemos can testify to that. You could have gotten off easily. Why put yourself in this situation with me, Lykeion?
You instructed me to provide a distraction on the boat because you know I'm a poor liar, Master Xanthos.
I knew about your plan and took action to help you. I must accept responsibility with you.
Besides, I do not think that what you did was unreasonable.
'A personal effect'? As if the officer believed that! She saw your courtesy as a way out, so she wouldn't have to prosecute her own people!

The relic that is at the center of the first cultural exchange between Sargon and Minos since the establishment of diplomatic relations was stolen, and damaged during its retrieval. This would be more than enough to start a war if we were in Leithanien or Siracusa!
I checked the harp. The damage was minor, only a crack on the pedestal. It can be easily repaired by filling and painting over the crack.
Under normal circumstances, I would have kept it untouched. But these are what we call extenuating. I can fix it so nothing looks out of place for the exchange ceremony in a few days.
Hah. You may be descended from Sargonian nobility, but you've inherited none of your ancestors' political acumen.
The Minoans are entirely at fault here. This is a perfect chance to punish the offenders severely, establish Sargon's authority and prestige, and—
I know you're proud of your family's 'military exploits' in Minos, Behnui Effendi, and to you this is like revisiting old stomping grounds, in a sense.
But times have changed. Padishah Khery appointed us to visit Minos with Kereseira 's harp, not to show off Sargonian glory, but to repair the relationship between our two nations.
As emissaries of Sargon, our priority is to carry out the exchange ceremony successfully, not our own obsessions and interests. Am I wrong?
This is the true harp of Kereseira.

The priestess of Endymion reaches out her hand to brush away a corner of the fence running around the burdenbeast pasture, pulling out a few overgrown blades of grass, revealing a harp built into the fence itself.
The simple instrument has nearly become one with the pasture. Not far away, the burdenbeasts leisurely munch on the radishes the priestess had brought along, humming in satisfaction.

...Its coloration has darkened, the paint on the base is mottled, and while the decorative elements are still there, their surfaces have burdenbeast bite and hoof-polishing marks. The whole thing looks like an ornament in the corner of the field.
I shouldn't question you... but this... I find it rather hard to believe...
You are a specialist in your field, and no doubt have a repertoire of techniques and standards to confirm it. You may examine it in detail.
If you say so. The wood used for the harp's body is definitely from the firs that grow near the site of old Athenius. As for its design, it's simpler than the one I brought.
The strings are far too new, though. These are metal strings, only popularized in the last few years...
The original strings snapped a long time ago. I replaced them recently.
I had also tried using traditional beastgut strings, but... No matter the type, the result was always the same.
Legend has it that the day Old Athenius fell, Kereseira's harp fell silent, never to sound again.
Metal, beastgut, once a month I would come here to try and play it, but I could never make it sing again.

...However you put it, it's the harp Kereseira used—
Assuming what you say is true...
The Sargonians aren't occupying Minos anymore. Why keep it hidden in a field?
Why, when I suggested holding the ceremony at the First Temple, no, even before that, when the Astynomos agreed to conduct the exchange for this relic—You're a priestess, you must have known.
Why didn't you bring it out earlier?
A harp that doesn't sing is merely a beautiful piece of wood.

It can be a fence in a field, or a toy for the burdenbeasts, but what it cannot be... is the centerpiece around which to gather everyone together for a ceremony celebrating the completion of a temple.
This makes no sense. Even the fake we brought plays notes that move the soul.
Which is why it is merely a fake. As for the harp before us, if you don't believe me, try it for yourself.
You may touch it. No need for reverence. I understand your hesitation.
If you can make it sing... If you can create a miracle, then you may consider it the harp you brought along, and use it to complete your trade.
I make you this promise, as the current keeper of the harp.
I... Sorry, but I've got one more question: why me? Sure, I need the real harp, but the First Temple also needs it...
I am a priestess of Endymion Temple, Kyría Mashayer.
We are the hierophants of revelation. For the people, we interpret dreams, decipher visions, and divine the future.
Sometimes, we will offer some comforts of our own accord, but most times, we will be passive, deferring to established facts and heroic wills.
Words flow from my lips, but they are beyond my control. Like how the things appearing before my eyes are not necessarily a pleasure to see.
We might call coincidence fate, but when true fate approaches, all our witticisms and pontification lose their meaning.
Do I have the power to allow the sun to shine, the rivers to flow, to dictate all from high to low?
You carry the harp of Kereseira—counterfeit though it may be—and it has appeared in Athenius. This can only be an omen. The events of the past few days stand as proof.
But what is most important is your character. It is because it is you, and not your fellow countryman, that I expect your spirit to resonate with the harp.
First, try singing heroic odes to it. Try playing it. Give your heart to it, and speak to it.
Then, perhaps, you really will be able to make it sing, and bring about a miracle.
She's finally gone... All that mysterious claptrap, sheesh...
What, ready to play already? You really believe her?
She is a priestess of Minos, and Pallas's friend. I doubt she'd lie to me about this.
Also... If we really can bring the real harp to the ceremony at the First Temple... It'll sure beat running all over Terra with a fake harp strapped to my back.
It doesn't really matter if this is the 'true fate' the priests speak of, or if it's some kind of test from the Minoan people, but I have to give it a try.
And how're you going to do that? All that 'give your heart to it', 'bring about a miracle' baloney, what's your methodology here?
Let's start by playing it.
Holy shit, that sounds terrible!
Not good. No matter what I try, it only makes these dull sounds.
Is the harp's structure damaged? But this kind of hand-held harp doesn't have a sound box... The issue is probably elsewhere.

I've already tried adjusting the tension of the strings, but no effect. Could it be a problem with some delicate internal component? But I don't have any tools to take it apart, inspect it, and reassemble it right now.
*sigh*...
What a pain in the ass.
I'm thinking we go back to stealing the fake harp. Worst comes to worst, I'll help you steal it.
Stealing it doesn't mean having to traipse around Terra... Well, we can still do that if you want. We could go to your Pepe girl for some funding.
But now that the real harp is right in front of me, making that choice now would be like running away.
Pfah. We can call it a tactical relocation, if you prefer.
Ever since I was little... The heroes in the tales my father told me, they never ran away.
Just my bad luck, having to take part in this worthless funeral.
A useless idiot who squandered every chance the Padishah gave him. This mongrel destroyed our family's reputation.
Hey, not in front of the girl.
What does it matter? I said all that to the man himself! All he did was smile. Didn't even have the courage to talk back.

Couldn't fight worth a damn, had zero skill in managing the family estates. He said he could appraise things, but he couldn't tell good gems from bad. All he ever did was buy a pile of ancient Minoan books. Who knows if that was even worth anything?
All he did was think about useless things like that!
Maybe he had the sort of romantic soul the poets sing of.
Romantic? Save it. Would you rather have romance or jewels?
You're asking me? It's not like I'm a poet myself. To me, a giant jewel is an expression of romance.
The day my father was buried, the nobles of the city spoke ill of him in front of me and my mother.
Mother said we were an ancient Sargonian family, and that we had to maintain perfect decorum, so all I could do was maintain an appropriate expression on my face.
I knew what they said may have been right, because he was a people-pleaser through and through, someone who amounted to absolutely nothing.
But I... still didn't want to hear them judge him as they pleased.
The heroes of the Minoan tales my father told me were brave, never shirking from their duties.
They would meet all sorts of obstacles on their journey, but they were always determined, forever confronting the challenges they had to confront.
I never really knew why... My father, he was... a weak man. Why did he love these stories...?
This passion of his, why did it never change him, or how he treated people? I simply don't understand.
Look, don't shoot the messenger here, but maybe some puny humans yearn for the things they don't have.
...Maybe.
But I've always remembered how clear and steady his voice was as he told me these stories.
I love those stories too.
Those heroes... Gentle, strong, resolute... Always walking their own paths. That's how I imagine them.
There must be some greater... purpose, or ideal, otherwise I don't know what meaning there is in life...
'A hero must face fate bravely when it comes, and carry their own burden.'
Father's stories... always had lines like that...
Those tales... Siopi... the hero who... suffered in silence... keeping her secrets... Kereseira... Heraces...
I... I need to think of a way...
As she sits out in the field, racking her brains, Titi falls into slumber. She dreams, a dream where she is but seven or eight.
Her father was already ill at the time. With one cold hand, he held her, while with the other, he flipped through the tome atop his knee, slowly reciting from it.
He never bought her children's books, or chose books specifically for her. He simply went with what he liked, but it didn't matter, because Titi liked those stories too.
Within those tales, the silent hero Siopi does not speak a word for thirty years, all to keep a secret. Kereseira, refusing to perform for outlanders, hangs her harp atop a laurel tree.
Perhaps it is the filter of dreams at work, but her father seems to tower over her, the slightly stooped back of her memories now ramrod-straight, his voice one of undeniable confidence.
'All of us have the chance to become a hero. When your duty falls upon you, you need only take it up with courage, Titi.'
Really? Can I? How can you be so sure?

It has been so long since she last saw her father, and she wants to ask him these questions, but it is only a dream, and it refuses to bend to her will. In the end, she could not ask what she wished to ask.
The gentle light of the twin moons falls upon the pasture, and upon the girl.
The land is painted in silver. A breeze blows, curling up the girl's ribbons, lifting the burdenbeasts' headscarves.
Even the antiquated, marred harp takes on a gentle radiance under the moonlight.
A teardrop slowly falls from the corner of Titi's eye. It rolls into the bushes, soon gone from sight.
Falling asleep after all that yammering. She didn't even wake me up. Pfeh.
It seems the little girlie needs her beauty sleep too.
Hey, what's with the crying?

You're always all about that stiff upper lip, always saying you won't cry, and now you're letting me catch you in the act? I need to keep some evidence for later.
Terminal... Photo mode... How the hell do you use this thing?
Ah, press here. Hee-hee!
*sigh*... Nothing interesting about a crying girl.
Forget it.
I'll help you out, you crybaby. Call it payback for the free meals these past few days.
You wanted a miracle, right? Easy as proverbial pie!
(Chews) Moo...?
Some real nice weather we've got today!

Gotta herd the burdenbeasts to the mountain pastures first, then since it's such a lovely day, I better repair the fence—
What the... The burdenbeasts...
How'd all the burdenbeasts get out?!
(Snores)
(Scratches ear)
Medjehtiqedti!

As if it wasn't enough that you went off by yourself first thing in the morning, now you're slamming doors and disturbing my nap! Have you repaired the harp, or have you been enjoying Athenius so much you forgot about your job?
There's no use trying to repair the harp...
Giving up so soon? You can't fix a tiny crack like that? What were you doing in all those classes you took in Columbia?
Just filling in the crack and painting over it isn't hard. But it's not just the harp that was damaged—
It was also your fragile bleeding heart?
It was also Sargon's reputation.
What?
Kereseira's harp was one of the best-preserved relics I've ever seen.
Wooden instruments are highly sensitive. The slightest blemish on the body could change its sound.
That was why we were very careful during the appraisal process and avoided any destructive tests...
I've heard it a million times at the museum—
'The ornaments, string materials and lacquer are typical of the Minoan style. Historical records indicate that it very likely belonged to Kereseira herself.'
But something didn't feel right when I was repairing it last night...
The wood chips that fell from the crack indicate that the material was mountain firs, the kind that ancient Minoans often used, but something was off with the cross-section texture.
The weapons of the heroes are made from ancient trees. They'll have some pretty deep texture.
No, it didn't look like the natural texture of wood. It was reminiscent of a certain moisture retention technique developed in Sargon.
I read about it in old books about instrument-making. Minoan plants are rich in moisture, while the climate in Sargon is generally dry.
Wood imported from Minos had to be specially treated to prevent them from cracking due to the dryness.
The cross-section I saw showed signs of such treatment.
You mean this was not Kereseira's original harp, but a replica made by Sargonians using Minoan wood? You sure you're not overthinking it?
I didn't want to believe it, but I just took the wood chips to a lab.
Testing revealed the presence of Sargonian resin, dating back about a hundred years...
Oof...

The harp looked perfect. It was virtually impossible to tell that it was a replica without destructive testing. I doubt Padishah Khery knew anything.
I have to tell the Astynomos. We can't give the Minoans a fake harp under the eyes of Kereseira's statue!
The exchange ceremony must be stopped!
Ambassador Medjehtiqedti's mission was to 'carry out' the exchange ceremony.
Behnui Effendi...

We made an oath to the padishah before we set out. How can you continue to call yourself the ambassador of Sargon, if you fail to retrieve the engineering data from the construction of the Three Poleis?
Yes, I have my duties as ambassador. As an archaeologist, however, I can't overlook such a mistake, much less intentionally participate in a lie.
That's why I say you have no political acumen. You see yourself as a museum curator, not a diplomatic envoy.
The important thing about the relic exchange ceremony is not the relic, but the exchange. Who cares if the harp is a replica? It still makes a sound.

You want to offer up a fake harp at the ceremony?
Absolutely not!
And what is your bright idea, Medjehtiqedti, without the authentic harp and a ceremony right around the corner?
In the halls of Menat-Ha'mait, you told Padishah Khery that you loved the legends of Minos, and wanted to see true heroism with your own eyes.
Is your idea of heroism clinging to your obsessions and bringing shame to your country at the very first cultural exchange between Sargon and Minos since the establishment of diplomatic relations?
Are you and your useless father one and the same?
He ruined your family, turned the name Nasutu Mashayer into a high society joke.
In his obsession with literature, he abandoned his duties, even his duty to his daughter, forcing her to chase the shadows of heroes in books, failing to find any heroism in her father.
This has nothing to do with him! I only love Minoan stories because—
'As emissaries of Sargon, our priority is to carry out the exchange ceremony successfully—not our own obsessions and interests.'
Your words, verbatim.
I'll find a way, before the ceremony, no matter what it takes. I can't let a fake...
Obstinate fool.
Very well. You can meditate while I act on your behalf and carry out the duty that the Sargonian ambassador was supposed to perform.
It not as if it changes anything. To the Astynomos and the guests, you're nothing more than a curious girl, an enthusiastic volunteer for the temple project while I negotiated with the Astynomos—
I am, for all due intents and purposes, the true ambassador of Sargon.
Hello. Maybe you don't remember me, but I've visited this ranch quite a lot lately. The herder, Melitta, knows me.
Please, stop leaning on the fence and let me through.
(Opens sleepy eyes)
It was not my intention to interrupt your nap. Perhaps you will enjoy this grass. The dew is still fresh on it.
I also brought some berries.
Well, I'm out of bribes. In that case...
Would you like a dream interpretation? Kýrios... maybe kýria.
(Brushes teeth on the fence)
Cauliflower is a girl. Berries are too sweet for her, and grass isn't filling enough. You need carrots to draw her attention.
If you find a burdenbeast has a fever and not even its favorite food can coax it to move...
Just scoop some water with a leaf and spray it on its horn to cool it down!
You really know your burdenbeasts, Melitta. I didn't know there were so many tricks to getting a burdenbeast to move.

Of course. They're not like beckbeasts, which are used to taking commands. A herder needs a hundred tricks to coax burdenbeasts into doing what you need them to.
It's this understanding that builds a bond between burdenbeast and herder.
Am I right, Cauliflower?
(Sways tail)
(Gets up and leaves)
Maybe it's a talent, to know one more way to get burdenbeasts to move than other herders.
Thanks for getting Cauliflower to move, Melitta.
No problem.
The priestess of Endymion Temple gracing us with her presence means that this place is blessed by the heroes. We couldn't be happier.
As long as it doesn't cause any trouble for you.
Of course not! All you do is admire the sights and look at the fence.
A little while back, I was concerned that a foal could slip through the cracks in the fences. A few days later, you filled the gaps with strong threads.
Just doing my part.
It was a very kind deed. I won't disturb you anymore; I have a few hooves to trim.
Kassandra leans towards the 'filled fence', where delicate threads stand in regular intervals, gleaming in the sun.
She plucks one of the threads, hearing nothing except the breeze blow over the pasture.
Of course it wouldn't make a sound. Not even a burdenbeast running into it did...
There isn't much time. When will you sing again?
I know what you're here for, Kýrios Behnui.
Rest assured that the relationship between our two nations is the highest priority. A little incident will not be allowed to affect it.
A little incident?
Xanthos came to Athenius five years ago, working as ferryman and manager of the crossing. He took in an apprentice, Lykeion, a former priest school student.
They are accused of attempting to steal from the room of Ambassador Medjehtiqedti. They are being held in custody, pending trial in accordance with the law.
Since the ambassador has elected not to press charges—
A justification to comfort the ignorant people by a naive girl moonlighting as supervisor of the rebuilding of the First Temple, not the decision of the honored ambassador of Sargon.
I see your views differ from Kyría Mashayer's.
I will, of course, take full responsibility for this matter as Astynomos. However, I would ask that the two of you come to an agreement as to how we deal with it.
You know what I mean, Astynomos. My view is that of the Sargonian delegation.
This tragedy, the theft and destruction of the harp, would never have happened if the exchange ceremony was not repeatedly delayed.

I have reason to suspect that you have intentions other than promoting the relationship between our two nations, that you have intentionally delayed the ceremony, and everything that took place did so on your orders.

I hear the position of Astynomos was newly created following the administrative reform of Athenius, and you were rewarded with the role because of your contributions to said reforms.
If something goes wrong with the first cultural exchange between our two nations since the establishment of diplomatic relations...
Then I congratulate you on being the only Astynomos who the people of Athenius will never forget.
The exchange ceremony is certainly my most important task. I admit I was negligent for not arriving at the scene promptly due to a busy schedule.
In that case, how would you define this incident... this 'theft and destruction of the harp'?
The brutal, barbaric, ignorant, baseless hatred of the perpetrators... the robbery and destruction of this symbol of friendship between Sargon and Minos was a most despicable act.
In my thirty years overseeing culture in Menat-Ha'mait, Sargon has received many letters from Minos requesting the return of the cultural artifacts that were removed during the occupation.
Shouldn't these plunderers of Minos's national treasure be severely punished, if you Minoans truly value it so much?
They will be tried according to Minoan law. As far as the punishment is concerned, though...
I have no intention of leaving the impression that Sargon is trying to bully the people of Athenius. We are emissaries of peace, after all.
This is a most sensitive time, however. Just because Sargon and Minos signed a truce five years ago doesn't mean that all is peaceful and well.
The padishahs are used to solving problems with force of arms. I would not give them a reason to get angry, if I were you.
Xanthos and Lykeion conspired to steal and deface a national treasure and sabotage the exchange ceremony...
Their actions are tantamount to treason.
They will be put on death row immediately.
Thank you, Astynomos. I look forward to the exchange ceremony in a few days.
Get in.
What's going on? It's not normal to transfer prisoners so suddenly, before we're officially sentenced.
I'm just following orders. Move quietly and behave yourselves... maybe there'll be a chance for clemency.
You really believed the Sargonian ambassador would protect the Minoans who tried to steal the harp.
We heard what she said to the officer. It would have been easiest to make the accusations while everyone was watching. But she didn't do it.
You can never tell what someone is thinking. Submit an official assessment of the damage, put pressure on the Astynomos and peace officers... there are many things that the Sargonian delegate can do.
Death row, eh? We're getting the royal treatment.
You should regret it, Lykeion. Destroying a hotel kitchen is nothing compared to the charges we now face.
All the more reason we had to take what's ours back from the bandits. The harp of Kereseira sings only for the stories of heroes. It will not, and must not, sing for a false peace.
I know your past, Master Xanthos. I may not have experienced war for myself, but I respect your decision. I couldn't let you risk everything by yourself, having known about your plan.
You saved my life two years ago.
It was your classmate from priest school, Hekádemos, who carried you down from the archery range after you were injured by an Originium shard.
I just took in a former student who wasn't willing to continue in the priesthood, that's all.
An Infected can never become a priest. It's divine work, communing with the heroes and guiding the way for the people. I couldn't do it.
As for Hekádemos, all he wants is someone to debate him...
Do not fool yourself, Lykeion. It will only make the pain worse, when you finally have to face it.
Master Xanthos?
My friend brought technology from the nomadic cities when he came to my homeland after receiving divine revelation. He built a village in the barren swamps with us.
The people in the village respected him a lot, but he always had a dark look on his face, late at night.

I lied to myself and tried to convince him that the river in his dreams wasn't red with blood; it was red because of iron that the river brought from upstream, and that the revelation was supposed to bring him to us.
But he didn't believe it...
He was prepared to go to war from the beginning. I was fooling myself.
River... hah... young people these days are foolish enough to fight the Sargonians to the death because of a dream like that?
And the two of you are here because you stole things from the Sargonians?
The harp of Kereseira belongs to the Minoans.
Even less worth it...
What truly belongs to the Minoans these days, after the Sargonians invaded a hundred years ago?
Hey, you're the one who invited me. Say something. I skipped the banquet celebrating the completion of the First Temple for you, you know.

You were a big part of getting the work across the finish line the day before the exchange ceremony. Tradition calls for a full day of celebration. The craftsmen began drinking and reciting poetry in broad daylight.
Not all of them hold their drink well. Guess the peace officers will have their hands full tonight.
Kantia from the moving crew, right? She's really taken to you after you helped her a few times.
Yeah... it's been like a dream, coming to Athenius.
Right you are.
First day in Athenius, you solved the dispute between the fruit farmers and craftsmen. Then you became the supervisor of the final phase of the temple rebuilding project.
And you chased Xanthos over multiple blocks after the harp was stolen.
Way more interesting than just escorting a relic, isn't it?
Yeah.
But my job was to ensure that the exchange ceremony goes smoothly, and promote the friendship between our two nations.
What's the matter, Titi? This isn't like you.
Pallas...
The harp of Kereseira we brought... it's a replica.
What?
I would never have known, if the harp wasn't damaged during my scuffle with Xanthos.
Now, my deputy, Behnui, wants to fulfill the padishah's mission by handing over the fake harp in the exchange ceremony... but I know that's not right.
The ceremony is tomorrow. Do you have a solution?
There's no good way to do this. We'd need a miracle.
I read through every relevant book I could borrow from the Bibliotheka of Athenius. I learned that the harp of Kereseira was lost to war a long time ago, and it hasn't been seen since.
It's likely that the Sargonian craftsmen who made this replica had only seen the blueprints, not the harp itself.
Could the true harp still be in Minos? Or...
Or it was destroyed by the fires of war long ago... I'm sorry.
There's no need for you to apologize.
What do you plan to do, if the true harp is nowhere to be found?
I can't publicly announce in my capacity as Sargonian ambassador that we brought a fake harp. It's more likely to lead to conflict than to reveal the truth.
A very reasonable concern.
And so, I'll have to stop the ceremony in my personal capacity.
What do you mean?
I'm getting rid of the fake harp before the ceremony can start.
You can't go in.
Look, I know a special visitor's permit is required to visit and deliver supplies. I just forgot.
Can you accommodate me this once? My old classmate went in a couple of days ago, all of a sudden. His family asked me to bring him some stuff.
Alright, to tell you the truth, he's pretty sick.
The Athenian jail has a doctor and basic medicine, but it's not as effective as the Rhodes Island stuff. He could have an attack while in custody, if he doesn't get proper treatment.
Save your breath, it's no use.
Alright. I didn't want to do this...
Here's my pass. Hekádemos, Grammateús to the Astynomos.
I am authorized to enter detention facilities, even bring in suspects for questioning. Open the door. This is an order. I will file the necessary paperwork later.
You want to see the two thieves who came in a few days ago?

Lykeion is not a...
Never mind. Yes.
You can't.
What? Why not?!
You are authorized to see prisoners held at the jail, but the two you're looking for aren't here anymore.

Were they transferred?
That is unusual. Has Kyría Athopoulou's Oripathy treatment system been implemented already?
Where were they transferred?
Don't bother, Hekádemos. I don't know who they are to you, but not even the Grammateús to the Astynomos is authorized to see inmates on death row.
Death row?!
Sorry, Kyría Athopoulou...
Does Lydia... I mean, does the Astynomos really not have the time?
I heard that Xanthos and his apprentice were arrested for theft.
They're my patients, and in a key stage of treatment, as the Rhodes Island clinic already reported.
Xanthos's Blood Originium-Crystal Density is particularly high, which could potentially lead to an acute attack of Oripathy. I would like to deliver medicine to them...
Look, I don't understand any of the medical jargon you're throwing around.
We are all very grateful to you for returning to Athenius, bringing back medical knowledge that you learned abroad and establishing an Oripathy clinic, but...
I shouldn't talk too much, but you're a good doctor, and the Astynomos' sister. You really should keep your distance from her for a few days at least, if only for the sake of your relationship.
Why?

The official line says the crime was theft, but the Sargonian ambassador came here a few days ago and said the thing that was stolen wasn't the ambassador's personal item, but an important relic for the exchange ceremony.
He put a lot of pressure on Lydia. They're probably going to be publicly charged with treason, once the ceremony is over.
Treason?! But according to Minoan law—
Most of the issues the Astynomos faces cannot be solved through legal procedures.
She wants a new, modern Minos, and she is willing to give everything for it. That's why we're willing to work for her.
Perhaps you're not aware, but she gave up her family inheritance, even chasing her other family out of Athenius after taking office...

She gave up her inheritance?
I thought the business failed...

This exchange of relics is an important part of her administration. There must be no mistake, at least not on the surface... even if it means sacrificing two lives. It's not like they're innocent, after all.
I'm sorry... I have to get back to work.

What is Lydia...?
Well, she wouldn't meet with me, and I doubt I can convince her, even if we did meet.
But Xanthos and Lykeion need to be tried fairly. I can't let Lydia make a mistake like this. What do I do?
Sorry, I was lost in thought—
Kyría Athopoulou?
You've been staring out the window, Astynomos.
Apologies, Hiéreia Kassandra.
Why not let Kyría Athopoulou in? I have to discuss the establishment of an Oripathy treatment system with her anyway.
Oh? I thought you wanted to get extra help to plead the case of the two Infected who were arrested a few days ago.
You know that ferryman, Xanthos, quite well. I believe you were the one who found him the job at the crossing?
The peace officers will deal with this theft. Neither of us need to worry about it.
I'm afraid I don't have that luxury. If this incident affects the trust between us and Sargon, then this incident will become more than a simple theft...
What?
It's my own business. It has nothing to do with a priestess of Endymion Temple.
Back on topic, Hiéreia Kassandra. The opening play for tomorrow's exchange ceremony has been decided. It is to be King Pertheus, a popular classic across Terra.
All the priests need to do is attend. There will be no need for complicated rituals.
A few weeks ago, I came to discuss the priest-directed ceremonial play for the completion of Kereseira's First Temple. I have not seen you at the Astynomos' Office since.

I apologize. My schedule is a busy one. I certainly remember. The exchange ceremony also requires an opening play, and we can kill two fowlbeasts with one stone this way. We need not bother the priests, however.

Excuse me, but the rebuilding of Kereseira's First Temple is a symbol of the people's spirit. Now that it is complete, tradition calls for a proper ceremony to invite the heroes to enter the temple and give Minos their blessings.
The council is not trying to usurp your administrative power, and the exchange ceremony does fall within your responsibility.
However, Minoan drama began as a ritualistic performance.
Every single detail is carefully arranged, from the props to the program to the performers. It's not enough to simply put on a popular play.
'Immortal legend'... 'witness of the oracle'... 'heroic relic'...
Do not think that I am completely ignorant of traditional ceremony, or that I'm one of those radicals who calls for every tradition to be abolished.
But even if you have your choice of the program and control over the troupe, there is still something missing from the traditional ritual that you want, isn't there?
...The Sargonian mission brought Kereseira's harp.
Oh? You do recognize the relic's ritual significance?
...What do you mean?
I'm neither an archaeologist nor a historian. As Astynomos, however, I understand every priest who sits on the council, including you.
You are the head priestess of Endymion Temple, and endowed with the divine gift of speaking on behalf of the heroes. Your knowledge of heroic epics puts most scholars to shame.
Yet you showed no interest about the harp of Kereseira that the Sargonian mission brought with them. You didn't even ask to see it.
I don't know why exactly, but I can tell that the harp the Sargonian mission brought was not the heroic relic that you knew.
The ceremonial play could not have been performed to begin with.
No, I already...
I have nothing against the council, Hiéreia Kassandra. If you present a legitimate heroic relic for the ceremony, I would be more than happy to give the choice of play back to you.
For the past century, however, the temples have mostly used crude replicas in place of true relics for your ceremonies. I don't need to tell you that the link between us and the heroes is weakening.
As a modern state, Minos can no longer rely on ancient legends to unite our people's spirit. No need to worry, however. The temples and priests will continue to exist.
You can continue to contribute to historical research and tourism.
It's getting late, Pallas. We should split up and make our own preparations.
Are you really sure about this, Titi?
Yes. Ever since Behnui learned the harp was a fake, he's had it under guard by our retinue. He's not letting me get anywhere near it.
He's definitely going to post more guards tonight so nothing unexpected happens, but it'll be a different story tomorrow when the exchange ceremony takes place.
He'll have to leave it exposed in public, which is where we'll find our chance...
Right.
But what comes next?
Next? The Sargonian government will probably give a formal apology about the whole affair, while sending more diplomats to discuss things with the Astynomos in private.
On the outside, it will look like a Sargonian mission taking a Minoan national treasure and fleeing, but the people in charge of both nations will know that all they lost was a fake.
That's not what my question was about, Titi. I want to know what will happen to you.
Me?
I'll take the fake harp and Mio and leave via the ancient route. Can't go back to Menat-Ha'mait, Padishah Khery's guards would definitely chase me across the entire city.
If I really do get caught, then it doesn't matter if it's by Behnui or Padishah Khery. Either one would most likely eliminate any inconvenient evidence, then push the blame onto Minos.

I'll have to keep running. Only then would Sargon's lofty nobles not dare look too deeply into this 'burglary' without authorization. What I will carry away with me is not just a fake harp, but the truth as well.
Titi...
Look, no trying to talk me out of it.
I'm all for better ideas, believe me. I don't really want to carry a big, heavy harp around Terra either.
Go home, Pallas. Let me be alone for a while. I'll see you tomorrow morning.
Fine...

lines.st: Titi leans against the back of a seat in the plaza, and looks up.
The night sky is always perfectly clear in Minos, a great boon to stargazers. It is this heavenly advantage that caused the ancient Minoans to associate their heroes with the positions of the stars.
When she was young, Titi's father would look up at the stars and tell her the stories of these heroes.
Some you can find in the history books. As for those with no source, perhaps they were simply cooked up by her underachieving bookworm of a father.
Though illness took him from this land prematurely, Titi still remembers clearly every story he told her.
Within those tales, those tales of Minos, will she find one of an exiled hero carrying a fake harp?
Countless stars sparkle silently in the dark sky. Titi does not find her answer.

lines.x: It's late, I clocked out a long time ago. Whatever it is, it can wait until my shift tomorrow—
It is I, Grammateús.
Kassandra?
Forgive me, I know you are resting at the moment.
Err... May I ask why you have deigned to visit me in the middle of the night?
I wished to know if the Astynomos has told you anything regarding the theft involving Master Xanthos and his apprentice.
I had called upon the Astynomos' office earlier to discuss the ceremonial play with her, and when I mentioned the case from a few days ago, she became evasive.
Ah, the Astynomos most likely has her own concerns. It's the exchange ceremony tomorrow, after all. She not only has to deal with the entire process, but also the Sargonian delegation.
Therein lies my worries.
If the delegation were to ask for the case to be re-examined in the name of bilateral trust, as the Astynomos is in charge of diplomacy, Lydia could hardly refuse.
I could tell she was a little tense while we were talking, and her side desk had piles of Minoan law codices and incident investigation reports.
She most likely is already having her hand twisted by the envoy. This means the charges against Xanthos and his apprentice will only become more severe, and...
And?
If it were not for your schoolmate's Oripathy nearing a crisis point, I do not believe there would be a Vulpo tail behind your sofa's armrest.
We finally meet, Lena Athopoulou, head of the Rhodes Island branch office.
Yeia sas, Kassandra...
Xanthos and Lykeion are her patients. She simply wished to give them their medicine while they are jailed, so she came to me for help.
Came to you for help? If this were any ordinary jailing, she should have no difficulty in sending medicine to convicts if she so wished.
It seems my conjectures were not too far from the mark. Lydia really is going to punish those two heavily to satisfy Sargon's demands. They have most likely already been moved to death row.
I never thought Hiéreia Kassandra, dream interpreter of Endymion Temple, would be so skilled in reasoning...
You should know, Hekádemos, that not even the Astynomos' Grammateús can visit prisoners on death row.
Unless you entertain some very dangerous ideas, such as...
A jailbreak.
I can't have Lykeion and his master dying in prison from an Oripathy attack. They might have done wrong, but the punishment is too severe.
You have not visited me tonight, Kassandra, and do not know about the sentencing on this case...
Could I ask you to help us keep things discreet? If all the heroes are up in the sky watching us, they would not sit idly by and allow something so ridiculous to happen.
Are you doing this to save lives as well, Lena?
I am.
I want to save my patients, and 'save' my sister. I understand she has her ambitions as Astynomos, but she has burdens to bear too, and she cannot keep making mistakes...
The eyes of the people will be on the exchange ceremony tomorrow. I've already obtained the prison blueprints through personal connections, and I have a route.
So long as we get them out, even if it's just to jab an emergency inhibitor into them, it'll be worth it...
A route is not enough. You need someone to buy you time at both the Astynomos' office and the prison.
I'll go find Lydia. As for the prison...
I can help you.
Kýrios, you said that nothing truly belongs to the Minoans... but how can that be?
The burdenbeasts we herd, you seen them before?
The scarves and veils draped on their heads we use to control what they see, are they not a Sargonian invention?
Ah, I am afraid you are off on the symbology of those scarves. Those are actually—
Haha, don't try to give me something like 'they are a subtle reference to the scarves the Sargonians forced us to wear during the occupation'.
What difference is there between ridicule and praise? Are they both not marks they have left on Minoan culture?
I fail to see how you can conflate the two...
Still being stubborn, are you? Then let us speak of the ground beneath our feet.
More than a hundred years ago, Minos spread its roots, drinking deep of this paradise of rivers, but now?

Padishah Malla, Queen of the Far Shore, gathered the engineers of Sargon and Minos alongside countless laborers, moving three poleis atop steel tracks. Athenius is far, far away from its former resting place.
That is obvious sophistry. Even if the Sargonians had not come, we would have eventually moved our cities onto nomadic plates ourselves. It's the inevitable march of technology...
Yes, well said! Have you spent time at Theros Temple, boy? However, there is one point you have overlooked: the very language we are using in this debate, right now, is 'mixed'.
The old tongue of Minos was eroded away during the occupation. The modern Minoan language the priests worked so hard to assemble, how many words do you think were taken from the nation you so hate?
You—
Enough!
You do not understand the man's meaning, Lykeion.
There is no removing the scars of war. A truce does not mean everything is in the past. We can no longer fight for the Minos in our hearts, for it no longer exists.
You, with the helmet. You're actually somewhat interesting.
Judging by your age, you could not have experienced the occupation yourself, but your words reveal a deep understanding of the period...
Do not waste precious time guessing at the history of strangers.
I'm just a tramp who doesn't care if he dies on the street or in prison. I once had a decent son, but he was stolen away by his dreams.
Dreams?
One morning, nine years ago, he ran out of his room, shaking me out of my hangover, telling me he had dreamed of a river, and that he should set off.
A river running red with blood. *sigh* I did not have to visit the priests of Endymion Temple to understand its meaning.
If that boy was not trying to get away from me, then he was thirsting for battle. He forgot what our ancestors had experienced, and forgot why his father is a useless, drunken gambler.
Divine revelation... A river red with blood...
...You are Ithakos's father?
Titi! Why're you back so late?! I thought you were busy 'fleeing from justice'!
We might actually have to flee from justice, Mio.
We what?! You spent all that time walking about to come up with this brilliant plan, and you want me to suffer alongside you?!
Take the fake harp far, far away, and place all the wrongs on one person's 'theft'. That's the best way to resolve this I can think of.
That's certainly not the best way, but it's definitely the most Medjehtiqedti way!
Huh?

Three weeks ago, when the delegation left Sargon, you said you wanted to experience the route Kereseira took when going to Athenius for the first time, so you followed the ancient route, even though it was a massive pain.

You could see Behnui's guards talking shit about you, but you didn't want to make a scene, and you were worried they wouldn't treat the relic right, so you took that big, heavy harp with you. By yourself.
And there was that time a few months ago in Menat-Ha'mait when you were helping that Pepe girl out of a bind. You went and poured an entire jug of wine all over yourself.
We both know you spent a long time grumbling to me about that afterwards, saying stuff like how a curator's salary isn't enough for all that dry cleaning.
But you didn't blame Pepe. After all, you believe this is what friends should do for each other.
If all your best ideas keep ending up with you taking on the burden yourself, then you should just quit already!
Pfft...
What's so funny, huh?!
Nothing, it's just that after hearing your words, I'm even more convinced I'm doing the right thing.
Like how Xanthos and Lykeion did not wish for a relic that is rightfully Minos's to be used as a diplomatic bargaining chip, so they stole it, bearing all the consequences.
I might not understand their true intents, but I believe their deeds are worthy of being recorded in the tales of the heroes.
Right now, I'm just a copycat. I don't dare assume I understand what makes someone a hero, but I want to write my own tale...
Even if I can't be considered a real hero, even if the protagonist of this story can only run away.
I never expected you to come to such a realization, Kyría Mashayer...
Kassandra?
Do you not blame Xanthos and his apprentice, who attempted to steal the harp? If it were not for their meddling, your cultural exchange would have gone smoothly, even if what you have is but a fake.
How could I blame them?
I have a feeling Behnui will try to seize the initiative to pressure the Astynomos into handing down an unjust sentence... but it's those two who reveal the truth.
If I can shift the focus of the issue onto myself by stealing the fake, and free both Xanthos and Lykeion, then it'll all be worth it...
Wait, how do you know the harp the Sargonian delegation brought is a replica?
Please come with me, Kyría Mashayer.
You really sure about leaving the village?
I'll be off at first light tomorrow.
The fields have already been replowed, there's plenty of seeds and grain, and the storehouse has also been fixed. As for the ferrying, I've taught a few of the young ones. Don't worry.
I'm worried about you, Xanthos.
You've never left the marsh before, and now you want to run off into the distance, all to find a stranger?
Ithakos is my brother. He's helped us all out before.
It's been an entire year. Maybe he's already headed back to Athenius... That's where he was from, it makes sense.
The things he agreed to do, he will do.
Once the war is over, he will return. He promised me.
Have you ever considered... if... if he's already...
He went off... to fight the Sargonians...
Then I shall avenge him. I shall avenge the Minoans.
Hey, don't get so worked up! You've got the wrong guy! I'm really not your Ithakos or whatever it was!
The one getting worked up is you, friend. I merely recognized the armor you wear.
It belonged to Ithakos, my brother. Before he left, every family in our village donated leather and iron for it. I was the one who made that set of armor presentable, and forged him his weapon.
F-Forgive me, I've been battling for so long now, big battles, small ones, and it's been a long time since I had some proper sleep. My mind's...
Here. There's still some calming herbal tea in the pot.
Can you tell me who you got this armor from?
Another guerrilla friend. We ran into a skirmish... He took an arrow, and before he died, he gave it to me...
But his name wasn't Ithakos.
Phew...
You see all these marks on the armor? It must've passed through a lot of hands to get that many...
You might not know this, but we guerrillas aren't like the Sargonian army. We're not well-supplied.
Everyone gathers up weapons, armor and food, which get given out depending on the mission's priority.
I can tell you that fallen guerrilla's unit number, and that'll get you where you need to be. Oh, right, let me give you the armor back first.
Keep it. It's still of use to you. Just telling me the way is enough, friend.
If you insist, but are you sure you want to search the mountains?
These mountains are our stomping grounds, and where we battle the Sargonians...
Then I'll join you.
You ever seen a battlefield before?
(Shakes head) Before all this, I had only ever held oars and farm tools.
But we have no other choice, do we?
Sorry, I don't really remember the smiling, long-haired Forte you mentioned.
As for these boots, I got given them when I joined the guerrillas... According to the friend who gave them to me, they belonged to a middle-aged guy who was missing a chunk out of his left horn.
There's a hole in them.
S-Sorry. They're a little small for me, and I haven't had the time to mend them.
Those Sargonians have been chasing us this entire time. If you hadn't shown up to support us in the nick of time...
Sit down.
Huh?
I'll help you mend them.
Na'sai kala... Wow, you've got big hands, but they're nimble... How long've you been one of us?
Four months. I came here to find someone, at first.
Keep the boots.
Fighting, marching, hiding, reorganizing. You bleed, you die, or you wipe your sword, then you jump into the next battle... Days like these are long ones.
Where's the man who gave you these boots? Just point me the way, friend.
I knew this letter carved on the corner of this shield meant something... You're saying it's short for 'Ithakos', and you carved it?
(Nods)
So you've spent half a year wandering the battlefields, staying with pretty much every one of our bands, just to find this guy?
It seems you don't have much you can tell me either.
Sorry, Xanthos. Also, I don't plan on giving this shield back to you.
Look at just how many blade marks there are on it! And these black marks... Sargonian Arts can barely make a dent in it!
I need to keep it. It'll let me protect more of our friends, maybe even keep me alive long enough to go home one day.
If that is the case, then I couldn't be happier.
Let the name 'Ithakos' stay by your side. Let our blood, our eyes, be witness to our victory.
This is where we part, my friend.
Just point me the way. It is all I ask.
Try changing hands. Ithakos was left-handed, which I kept in mind when I made this Arts unit for him.
I knew it! I knew something felt off about the power levels!
...It's already been a whole year, Xanthos.
Time flies, as swift as a melted river.
You're already the most famous guerrilla in the mountains. You've fought the most fights, walked the most battlefields. Everybody sings of your heroism...
Are you really going to keep searching for your brother?
I have no reason to stop.
Like how you will not turn and leave the battlefield.
Hah, you're right.
Your accent. You're from Lachedamon?
I sure am. Well, more specifically, my hometown is on the route the nomadic city follows. It's a river valley, absolutely beautiful in spring.
I've just told you about my hometown, the village Ithakos and I founded together. You should tell me more about yours.
We have been losing this war for over a hundred years. We do not know how it will end. We should remember each other's hometowns... We should remember each other.
I'll be more than happy to, my friend.
I continue my search tomorrow. Just point me the way, friend.
Why must I suffer the indignity of being tied to this tree, Minoan?
Like I said, answer my question.
Ithakos, Ithakos, Ithakos! Always about Ithakos!
Fine, fine, tozz feek, I've had enough... Two years ago, my troops met with Minoan guerrillas near here. They were escorting some wounded.
I remember their ensign, matches the unit you mentioned.
We wiped them out. That Ithakos you keep talking about should've been one of them.
You happy now? Did you seriously set up an ambush for the sake of a battle two years ago, so you could capture me and have your revenge?
You're mad, Minoan, an absolute lunatic!
You might not believe me, but this is all just a coincidence.
Before today, I was not thinking of finding Ithakos, or killing his enemies.
This battle is no different to the ones of the past year, no, the past century.
You invaded land that doesn't belong to you, Sargonian, and we have no choice but to defend our home.
I have simply become accustomed to asking everyone I meet: 'Have you seen Ithakos? Have you seen that smiling, long-haired Forte? He's from Athenius, but he has a south country accent.'
Such a thing truly does mean I am, as you say, 'mad'.
I have found his armor, his boots, his shield, his Arts unit... Everything he left behind on this wooded mountain, I have found.
I have found him.
I have never once thought you invaders would remember the name of every Minoan you met. Your padishahs hardly care about what every line in your books of merit actually means, do they?
That said... Ithakos. Remember this name.
It is our passion, our love, and your crime, your punishment.

Now, tell me.
Where was that battle, exactly?!
Which of these firs was watered with the blood of Minos?!

lines.st: Xanthos looks up, and sees countless Minoan firs, standing tall. The wind blows, and the entire forest rustles.
This land, these woods. They remember many things, but they do not speak.
His brother, his friend, the youth who followed his revelation and left home. He died here, somewhere. Everywhere.
Ithakos, countless Ithakoses, have left not even bones, all for the sake of the land beneath their feet.
Xanthos lifts the giant oar in his hand. After an entire year, it has forgotten the river's burble. Now, all that can wet it is the blood of his enemy!

lines.x: Did you avenge Ithakos?
No.
Just as I was about to smash in the head of that Sargonian with my weapon, my friend stopped me, while the bugles cried out.
You never told me the campaign you experienced was...

The final battle before Minos and Sargon signed a ceasefire. Minos, which had lost for a hundred years,
hah, had finally won.
That friend of mine desperately held back my hand. He had a broken leg, yet he could still bring such strength to bear to rein me in.
I turned, and saw his face full of tears, and his eyes full of fear... Not towards the Sargonians, but towards me.
I knew what he was so afraid of.

He was afraid I would destroy this hard-won 'peace'. He was afraid I would bring about new conflict, that I would extend this cruel war. He was afraid he would become like Ithakos, forever unable to return home.
My pent-up rage and grief had nowhere to go. All I could do was watch that Sargonian officer kneel on the ground, numbly listening to the bugles. The same numbness I felt...
It was that moment I knew the war will rage on in our bodies. It had begun, and it could never end.
You are right. The Minos in my heart no longer exists. It died on the battlefield many years ago, alongside the Ithakoses.
But I will not allow the enemy to defile our land again. Not even if they had built this city.
'Not even if they had built this city'... So, even the textbooks say that nowadays?
What do you mean?
Ithakos went to your village, so full of passion, unlike me. He must have taught you many building techniques.
Indeed. He renovated nearly every important building in the village.

His techniques were passed down from his ancestors. During the occupation, my own grandfather was pressed into service by the Sargonians to build the nomadic cities, thanks to his mastery of engineering.
What?
The mobility layer of this Athenius might just be his handiwork.
Forgive me, I was far too rude to you earlier. I...
Do not bow your head for that, boy.
I am not the engineer who built the nomadic cities, I don't have proof of how much our ancestors toiled building Athenius. All that data was taken away by the Sargonians.
The Sargonians wanted the Minoan engineers' data... but why?
An engineer's notes will always have something more than just jargon and modification blueprints.

He had to keep track of the quantities of building materials, while coordinating transport and construction teams, so he was a meticulous planner of manpower, and remembered the names of every worker.
Not Sargonian workers, but the names of those from Minos, and where they came from, and how the Sargonians forced them to do their bidding.

Minoan workers? As I recall, current mainstream thinking is that Padishah Malla did not use any Minoan workers for heavy labor while the Three Poleis were being built.
Do you mean to say...?
History was not written by us, not even our final victory.
But there is evidence of wrongdoing, evidence that should be shared with the public. Do you remember where these engineer's notes are?
How should I know? Ask the Sargonians... Ah, but those Sargonians are probably dead by now, haha...
Ask the Sargonians... The construction of the Three Poleis...
No!
What is it, Master?
The Sargonian mission is not using the exchange to obtain technical data, but rather, the notes detailing proof of the Sargonians' sins!
Oripathy suppressants for two. A few days have passed since their last injection. Give them a shot as soon as you see them to prevent an acute attack.
The rest are standard medicine. This is a painkiller, these improve cardiovascular functions, this is for blood pressure, and this is for regulating blood oxygen levels.
Xanthos seems to be hiding his illness and may be unwilling to take medicine. Bring it anyway, and make him carry it any way you can.
The Oripathy suppressant is mandatory. The rest depends on him.
Alright. Is this all for Xanthos? What about Lykeion?
Lykeion? Suppressant injections are the only thing that can be done at his current stage.
It's that bad?

It's not so bad. No need to worry.
There's water and cheese pita for three in this bag.
I'm not going on a picnic...
Bring it anyway. You might get hungry.
Don't worry, I also got you some good stuff.
This box contains soporific incense pills. Just in case.
Make sure you stand upwind when you use it, or you'll put yourself to sleep.
Kyría Athopoulou... I thought we had already made the arrangements?
Kassandra has obtained the schedule of the jailer shifts.
The Astynomos' Office has a duplicate key for the jail door and shackles, and I made my own duplicate—
It was careless of Lydia to leave them on the table.
There hasn't been a prison breakout in ages, so security isn't particularly strict. I'll find the right time, go in, get them out. Shouldn't run into any issues.
There's no need to be overly nervous.
I know... it's what happens afterwards that I'm thinking about.
You're worried you can't distract Lydia? Or are you worried about your future?
While I'm the one who's actually carrying out the prison break, Lydia is likely to find out about your participation.
It may obstruct your company's efforts to develop your Athenian office...
Xanthos and Lykeion are my patients, even though one of them isn't the most cooperative.
When I made the decision to become a doctor, I also made the decision that I will not leave any patient behind.
There are some things that have to be done, no matter the price.
It's Lydia whom I'm really worried about... I need to talk to her.
Don't worry about me. But you...
Don't worry about me.
There's nothing left for me here. The only reason I didn't quit was that I was waiting for Lydia to fire me so that I could collect my severance—
Not getting it is my only regret.
I'm more excited than anxious about leaving this nomadic city for broader horizons.
I heard you were once forced to leave Athenius, Kyría Athopoulou. Perhaps you understand the feeling.
...Yes, I do.

Your situation now is much like mine back then.
You—
Things will get better for you, I promise.
Just as it got better for me.
We need to find a way to get out of here.
We can't just sit here and wait.
Yes, we need to get Lena to look at you. Your health—
My health is not important.
The relic exchange ceremony takes place this afternoon.
If the Sargonians are allowed to take the engineering notes from the Three Poleis, they will be able to dictate how the history is told.
This must be stopped.
Hold it, Xanthos.
You have Oripathy. Many on the Sargonian border contracted the disease in the conflict, just like you.
...Yes.
Many lost their homes, their livelihoods. But you found your footing after coming to Athenius.
You've got a new life. Why do you want to get involved in such things?
Maybe it's because I have Oripathy.
I don't have much time left. I must spend what little I have on things that are more meaningful.
Then you're just another Minoan bound by the heroic dream.
I don't think about those things. Oripathy saps the heroic spirit. I have not had the heroic dream ever since I left my homeland.
Is that so?
Who could have imagined...?
Ithakos found a good friend out on the borderlands.
The guards change shifts in fifteen minutes. That's your opportunity to get out.
Go out the door and turn left, then take the second right turn, and go right every time you see a crossroads after that. You shouldn't see any guards if you get out within five minutes.
You'll eventually find yourselves in the kitchen, which should be empty at this hour. You can get out from there.
Oh, right, the shackles. Come over, I'll unlock them.
Why...?
Why don't I leave? Well, my situation is a little different—not everyone in this prison is scheduled for the death sentence. Some need to be locked up until the day they die, like me.
And why should I leave, when I don't have to worry about room and board? What difference is there anywhere on Terra?
Or did you want to ask why I made these preparations? Came in useful right now, didn't it?
There.
...Thank you.
You're welcome.
I don't know what you're planning when you get out, but I doubt there'll be a place for you in this city anymore. I don't suppose we'll meet again.
Ithakos died for his heroic dream. Divine revelation had shown his future. None of us could stop it.
But you're not the same. You haven't received your divine revelation yet. Perhaps... in the distant future...
Until then... live well.
All this stone, these incomplete statues, and those scaffolds—why are they still here?
I knew your people are lazy, but I didn't expect you to be so shockingly inefficient.
And where is that Astynomos of yours?! She's the one who should be receiving me here!
We've already discussed the ceremony with Titi.
A space has been cleared for the ceremony, the speakers and spotlights have been set up, and signs have been placed around the audience seats.
We've completed all the necessary preparations. The things that you noted are not.
Medjehtiqedti? She's nowhere to be seen.
I'm in charge of the Sargonian mission now, and it's MY needs you must attend to.
And I think that your preparations are entirely inadequate for a ceremony as important as the exchange of relics between Sargon and Minos.
