As you said, it is an important ceremony, and we will do everything in our power in the remaining time to make the venue as perfect as possible.
However, I must also put a question to you—
What is the condition of the relic that you are handing over, which is of paramount importance to Minoans and the First Temple—the harp of Kereseira?
I heard that it suffered a serious fall a few days ago at the dock, and that your delegation intends to repair it. Are the repairs complete?
And I'm curious—while I don't know how your padishah and Lydia came to such an agreement, does the agreement include an appraisal as well?
You're overstepping, Pallas.
We have an accord with the Astynomos. This will be a massive occasion. She informs me that priests from Lachedamon and Corinia will be in attendance as well.
This is unprecedented publicity for you and your temple. You should focus your full efforts on the preparations.
You do not need to worry about anything else.
You're right. I look forward to the event in the afternoon.
That Sargonian sure has his nose in the air, doesn't he? I'm surprised you didn't punch him.
Or are your plans in motion, and you're just watching the clown make a fool of himself?
Clown? Are you talking about yourself?
You've been in Athenius a little too long, Corinian.
The apples that the Athenian orchards are exporting to Corinia have already been packaged into boxes. Why are you still here?
I'm a priest of Corinia. It's my job to attend certain important occasions, in addition to business dealings. I'm here at your Astynomos' invitation.
Besides, to a Corinian, a transaction that has already been settled belongs in the past. The important thing is to seek opportunity for the future.
Is that why you keep sniffing around like a musbeast in a ditch? What favors did Lydia promise you?
Is there anything you won't sell for the right price? Your dignity, your conscience, even your soul?
Everything is for sale. If you can afford it.
Rather than pay so much attention to me, I suggest you spend it on the people who are not here.
You mean the Astynomos? It's true that she and her Grammateús should be here, but neither are.
Perhaps they've been sidetracked by private matters.
It's someone else whom I'm talking about. As the Sargonian said, the Astynomos has invited priests from the other poleis.
The guest from Corinia is standing in front of you. But where is the priestess from Lachedamon?
Think about it. A Lachedamonian, stubborn, obstinate, serious barbarian, for whom violence is the only answer to any problem.
As far as I know, this particular Lachedamonian was born in Athenius, but she was taken to Lachedamon from a young age, for reasons unknown, and grew up in a temple there.
She wears certain elements of Athenius, perhaps as a kind of memento, but she was brought up with an orthodox Lachedamonian temple education.
The three-time reigning champion of the Lachedamon Pankration, and the youngest priest in the city's history, having graduated top of her class.
I'm curious... how would such a remarkable figure react, if she saw an unexpected scene of chaos?
This is the real reason why I stayed.
Good timing. Take over for me for a moment. We need someone here who can be in charge.
I'd better find Titi. I coaxed a little bit of information out of the Sargonian deputy. Apparently, her plan to steal the harp was unsuccessful. I'm worried for her.
If she failed, I'll spill the beans at the ceremony... we have to discuss how to proceed after that.
The problem is that I don't know where she is. She wasn't at the hotel...
She's at the burdenbeast ranch on the outskirts.
Ranch? Why?
Go and find her. Maybe you'll witness a miracle, and find that all those things you were worrying about are not a problem anymore.
Don't forget what I said to you last night.
I think someone's coming, master.
Did we take a wrong turn?
I don't think so.
Just one person, I think.
We've come too far to give up now.
Go hide in that corner. When they pass, knock them out and throw them into an empty cell.
Okay.
Cover their mouth and don't let them make a sound.

Hm... how should I talk to him when I see him?
Hm...
Whoa?!
Mmph—mm—ouch—who are you—ugh—huh?
What are you doing here?
Phew, finally got out. Let's hide here for now.
Thank you.
Don't mention it! Just glad to get you safely out of there.
The next part is the hard part... I have to take you to the ancient route. Kassandra has your documents and luggage waiting there.
We'll need to be careful and avoid drawing attention to ourselves. Let's hope it takes a while for the jailers to notice...
Kassandra...

She was very worried about you. Did you want to say goodbye to her before leaving? I can understand.
But she's at the First Temple right now, where all eyes are on. It will be difficult.
Let me think...
Movement over there.
The jailers found out so quickly?!
I don't think so... sounds like the crying of a beast.
Burdenbeast!
What are burdenbeasts doing in the city?!
Moo—!
The burdenbeasts are coming straight at us!
Master!
Titi said the harp that the Sargonians brought was fake.
You're not surprised?
I'll talk to her about it later. It's you whom I'm looking for.
You've been relying on her to handle the First Temple's matters for you these past few days.
I thought we already talked about this. Who's in charge is not important.
But she can't replace a priest.
Only the priests have full knowledge of the heroes that a temple venerates, the ceremony to invite them, and the ceremonial plays.
Only a priest can gather the spirit of the heroes during a performance.
You're the only one who can do it for Kereseira's First Temple. No one can do it in your stead, not even me.
Stop running, Pallas.
I know. I wasn't going to run from my responsibility, the day I made the decision to return to Minos.
She does worry, doesn't she? I should buy her a round of drinks after everything's done.
What did she discuss with Titi last night, anyway? I forgot to ask...
And what's this 'miracle' that she was talking about?
I finally understand the meaning of the idiom, 'suffering is the price of wisdom.'
I didn't feel that way when I was a priest, but now I fully understand how irritating their habit of speaking in riddles is.
What exactly is Titi doing at the burdenbeast ranch, anyway?
Pallas! Look out!
It began as a distant growl, as Pallas rides at a steady pace on the street.
Then she sees the distant movement close in rapidly, becoming a herd of rampaging burdenbeasts.
Dozens of burdenbeasts charge through the streets with unstoppable momentum, leaving peace officers and herders in the dust, sending pedestrians and stalls flying.
They wear the scarf of the burdenbeast ranch on the outskirts—
Where Kassandra said Titi was at?
Something seems to have frightened these normally docile creatures, as they form a tidal wave that rushes in the opposite direction from the ranch.
The burdenbeast at the rear of the stampede... there's something on its horn—
Is that... a harp?! Could it be... Kereseira's...?
Is this the 'miracle' that Kassandra was talking about?
Does a Leithanian thin-tailed grass have single leaves or compound leaves?
No idea.
There are several methods for extracting red velvet mallow essence. Which one is most effective at retaining its soothing properties?
No idea.
This boronia that you planted...
All I did was help you shovel the soil. I have no idea why you plant it, or how.
You'll be tested on all of these things, Lydia.
The Athopoulou family has venerated Flora the Hero for generations, conducting plant-related work such as those associated with perfumes, medicinal herbs, and plant breeding.
The elders are very serious about this legacy. You'll have to participate in the traditional heir selection ceremony in a few years. You need to remember these things.
Everyone knows you're the heir to the Athopoulou family.
Uncle Steros can't stop talking about how proud he is of his daughter. He'll declare you the Flora of Athopoulou any day now.
I know my father hasn't been fair towards you...
He's already shown as much kindness as possible by taking me in after my parents passed away. Besides, you know I have no interest in plants.
But father has scheduled a test for next week. You'll be punished again if you fail...
I peeked into Uncle Steros's notebook. The thyme harvest has been disappointing. I expect he'll be going to Corinia soon, in order to find suppliers to meet the shortfall.
Corinia? He didn't say anything—
Crouching in the garden again, Lena? ... I'm going on a week-long trip. Take care of yourself and Lydia while I'm away.
Wow...
See? There are certain things that I'm good at, just like you're good at plants and perfumes.
*Pant* *pant* Help! Somebody! Anybody!
Some skata tradition! Why did Athopoulou have to do the selection ceremony out here in the middle of nowhere?
The one who finds the rarest plant is the one favored by Flora? Ridiculous!
Someone's hurt! We need a doctor... and emergency inhibitors!
You'll hurt your throat shouting like that, Lydia... carrying me is hard enough as it is, put me down...
Well, you can help by throwing away that big heavy plant! There's no one more suited to being the heir to House Athopoulou, with or without it!
I know, it doesn't mean anything to me now.
The Flora of Athopoulou cannot be Infected. Father will never accept it. I'll probably have to leave Athenius as soon as we get back.
You don't need the approval of that bastard, if he's so cruel as to cast aside his own daughter.
But the family needs an heir.
Write it down, Lydia. Boraginales, Boraginaceae, Cynoglossoideae, Myosotis...
What do you mean... don't tell me about it, Lena! I don't want to know!
Listen to me.
It's a common plant across Terra, but it's only known to grow around marshes, and it has never been found in Minos.
It reminded me of you.
You dislike the family tradition, and you have no interest in plants or herbs, as unlike Athopoulou as can be... but you're observant, and you're a good communicator. You'll make a good heir.
That's your place. I don't want it...
Let's make a bet, shall we? Just like when we were little.
Take care of this rare plant for me. If the elders have no issue with my condition, then I'll never make you come with me to that 'boring' garden ever again.
But if father shows disgust at my wounds... you must claim to have found this 'big heavy plant'.
Don't worry, Lydia. No one will bully you anymore, not even my father, once you become the heir.
With you, the Athopoulou family will be in good hands.
Lydia...
Why did you ask me to come to this public garden? I don't think there's any need for us to reminisce about old times.
If you have something to say, say it. I have a lot waiting for me to do.
The exchange ceremony is about to begin. Besides verifying the guest list, making sure the ceremony goes smoothly, I must—
You must announce to Athenius that Xanthos and Lykeion have been found guilty of treason for the intentional theft and destruction of a Minoan national treasure?
Not satisfied with turning from perfumer to doctor? Are you making another career change to defense attorney?
This is a critical juncture for the relationship between the two nations. I cannot allow an ill-planned, impetuous theft to destroy everything.
A century of war didn't destroy Minos. Are you working for the interests of the people of Athenius, or to protect your own position as Astynomos?
A cliched moral dilemma...
Of course I want to protect my position as Astynomos.
How do you think Rhodes Island managed to establish an office in Athenius in such a short time?
Priests buried in ancient tomes, the chaotic council, the convoluted voting process... you would have waited an entire year, if you followed the 'traditional process'.
Kereseira's First Temple and 'following the old ways'? What a joke.
How could the place be ready for today's exchange ceremony, if the Sargonian girl hadn't intervened and pushed the final phase through with modern, effective project management?
So you judged everything with your scales, not caring that the weights you are using are real people?
Do you believe that everything must give way to 'people', then?

I'll do everything I can to find a solution, before things get to the point of no return. My colleagues and friends will walk together with me, no matter how treacherous the path. As for your question...
I'm a doctor, Lydia. It's the basis of everything I do.
The ground beneath them trembles. The sound of howling approaches.
Dozens of burdenbeasts jump across fences and benches, stampeding through the public garden.
The herd steps upon the spacious grass, running towards the tall building in the distance, seemingly quite sure of their destination.
In the frenzied stampede, a young burdenbeast is drawn to something to the side.
Two solemn human figures reflect in its wet, curious eyes, but it quickly loses interest in them, catching up with the herd and leaving only drifting flower petals behind.
The scent of flowers surrounds the two Athopoulous.
Their keen sense of smell, honed by rigorous training from a young age, allows them to easily identify the damaged flowers, but not the true thoughts of their counterparts.
Neither of us could ever change the other's mind. It's been this way since we were little.
But there's a question I always wanted to ask you.
What makes you so 'great', Lena Athopoulou?
Lydia...?
You approached a girl that all the Athopoulous disliked, took her to your favorite garden, and taught her knowledge about plants that she had no interest in, all of it on your own.
You gave her the plant that determined the family's heir, thought the family was in good hands, and left Athenius, again all of it on your own.
You never cared about what I was good at, and it never occurred to you that I stayed with the Athopoulou family, not out of any sense of responsibility, or for the heir's position...
I didn't dislike the garden, Lena.
But you chose not to keep it. You chose to take it back from the family after it fell on hard times, turning it into a public facility.
That's our fundamental difference.
You hate the name Athopoulou as much as I do, but you're always looking backwards, even trying to get it back, while I never look back.
I know you asked me to come here for the sake of the two prisoners. Maybe you're trying to delay me, or maybe you have some other plan in mind.
After I became the heir to the family, it took me another two years to cast the mantle aside. Only now do I have the chance to do what I really wanted to.
As you said, though, no one bullied me during that time. I played along with your game for ten minutes. It's my final gift to you.
I owe you nothing now, Lena.
I see.
The grey-haired Vulpo gazes at Lena's departing figure. She had imagined their reunion before Lena returned to Athenius; afterwards, she had imagined their parting.
Never before had she been so acutely conscious that they were going to part ways forever.
The woman before her is no longer her foster sister who took care of her, the Lena who gently but insistently instilled botanical knowledge in her.

Lena is a Rhodes Island operator now. She has her own dreams and aspirations, her own home that she will go back to, her own family that loves and supports her. None of it has anything to do with Lydia.
Suddenly, a thought without any basis whatsoever comes to Lydia's mind—
Even if Lena remains in Athenius, and continues to work to develop an Oripathy treatment system, and continues to have dealings with the Astynomos' Office...
This will be the last time she sees Lena ever again.
Phew... we should be safe from the burdenbeasts here.
Where's Master Xanthos?
I think I saw him head towards the First Temple. To say goodbye to Kassandra, probably. We'll find a way to meet up with him, don't worry.
Why are there stampeding burdenbeasts on the street? Aren't you the Astynomos' Grammateús? Shouldn't you be finding out what's happening?

There are already peace officers heading that way. They'll take care of it.
This stuff's none of my business anymore—
I resigned.
To rescue Master Xanthos and me from prison? That's not necessary. We—
You wanted to say you could have gotten out on your own, right? Well, it doesn't matter. I was planning to quit anyway.
What exactly is your plan, Lykeion?
I don't know what you're talking about.
Please, you never passed your classical drama courses and it shows.
You had the exact same expression on your face when you submitted your withdrawal from priest school after contracting Oripathy.
It was the day before the debate contest, and we were practicing archery as usual.
You were a much better archer than I was. I was pissed and intentionally fired a shot into the distance with your bow. The accident happened when you were looking for the arrow...
I carried you down the mountain and ran into Xanthos. He ferried us to the best Oripathy doctor available, but you got infected in the end.
And so we missed our seventh debate contest that would have broken our 3-3 tie.
Is this really the right time to talk about this?
Since when did Minoans need the 'right time' to debate? In the halls of Theros Temple, on the streets with drunkards and gamblers, everywhere is fair game.
I thought you would only be down for a day or two, and we would be back in the competition in a couple of years. But you just ran away.
We never decided the winner all these years. It's about time you gave me back this last debate, Lykeion.
What's the topic?
Let's say... whether an Infected can become a hero.
Hekádemos!
It's inappropriate.
Why? It's a topic you're intimately familiar with.
'Oripathy saps the heroic spirit.' It's conventional wisdom that we all take for granted. When a child questions it, he or she is quickly put down by the elders who cite various heroic legends.
There are no Minoan heroes with Oripathy in the legends, no depictions of Originium crystals on the temple statues.
Therefore, you, a student who aspired to be a hero, gave up on becoming a priest before the rules had a chance to become an obstacle...
Your every deed is representative of your position on this topic.
Don't bother trying to provoke me. We both know there's no chance I'll lose this debate.
I thought about starting over, and Master Xanthos encouraged me. But I realized that someone who knows he has no future cannot commit himself to anything.
Regular suppressant injections, a regular lifestyle, and the newest Rhodes Island treatment may make the patient live longer.
But I'm not satisfied with just living. Yet it seems to be the only thing I can do, and I feel nothing but shame about it.
The worst thing about Oripathy is not the physical torment, but the inevitable deadline that it sets for all dreams and aspirations.
Even if Oripathy doesn't sap the heroic spirit, even if you could become a priest after becoming infected, even if the door to becoming a hero used to be open once—
That door is closed now. Do you understand?
I see.
In that case... I win this last debate.
Who said the debate's over?
What?
You've demonstrated your position with your actions. I shall do the same, Lykeion. I will make you a hero of Minos.
Have you any idea what you're saying?
There's no other way to get you to admit defeat, is there?
Xanthos could encourage you, or the deeds of other Infected could move you, but deep down, you would not admit that being infected or not has nothing to do with one's heroic spirit.
In that case, the simplest way is to make a hero out of you.
I've already quit my job. Wherever you go now, I'll follow, and do everything to make you keep on living, make you meet more people, make you be a part of important things.
What is this, some newfangled punishment? What do YOU get out of it?

You know I was never the most motivated guy. I grew up to be that guy who always punches out exactly on time and never breaks a sweat when he could help it. Heroes are way too far away from my life, and I don't want to become like them, no matter how glorious it might seem.
I'll make a hero out of you, a true hero, one whom everybody recognizes. As for what's in it for me—
I get to win our last debate. That's all I want.
...You're out of your mind.
Thanks for the compliment. I'll take that as a yes.
Oh, but you're late-stage, aren't you? That's a pretty big disadvantage for me... is it true that you have difficulty seeing and you get dizzy easily?
What are you talking about? I'm just mildly infected. I don't have those kinds of symptoms.
Mildly infected? But you keep going to the Rhodes Island clinic, Kyría Athopoulou had to pay you a home visit, and you rammed the hotel kitchen because you couldn't see the stop sign clearly.
You've got it all wrong... it's Master Xanthos whose condition is serious.
Xanthos?
We never had a plan, Hekádemos.
Master Xanthos broke out of prison with me because he wanted to do something that was worthwhile to him, while he still could.
Even if the price is his life.
You shouldn't have come here, Xanthos. People are gathering at the temple. You might have been able to avoid the crowd when you came, but it's going to be much harder to leave. Go, now.
I came to say goodbye. I don't think we'll meet again.
Whether the farewell is face-to-face or not doesn't matter. What matters is that you're at peace. Go back to your homeland, Xanthos.
Go and see that old river. Even if it has dried up, the melted snow always flows down from the mountains. Maybe another water source nourishes your homeland now.
I know. The priestess of Endymion watches over all our souls.
You always give us false hope.
I remember the day you first came to Athenius. It was a rainy night, and you walked into my temple with Ithakos's bloody chiton.

You were downtrodden and lost. But you told me about your promise with him when he went to war. You said you imagined yourself ferrying him to the temple on the other side of the city on the Delphi Canal.
It was the flame of hope, faint and weak, but burning nonetheless. It is not a false hope that I instilled. It is a real hope, burning inside your heart.
It was real.
It still is, only that I had hoped you would nourish it with life, but it grew into something else as you ruminated over it.
Going back to your homeland, you might remember the past that was even longer ago, and let your pain be soothed by the voices of the past in the never-changing winds.
What about the Sargonians?
I know you think about the exchange ceremony. Divine revelation has already made arrangements.
When the harp that belongs to Minos sings its song again, the people will once again be united, and Kereseira shall drive out the outlanders.
Divine revelation again.
There was always a question that I wanted to ask.
What IS divine revelation? How does it get people to willingly go to their deaths?
It's the power of faith and belief. It's the accumulation of a thousand years of dreaming about heroes, the thing that makes Minoans Minoan.
We do not have 'gods', in the sense of the word that the outlanders use. Our gods are our heroes, each and every one of them.
Our hero Kereseira was once an ordinary woman.
It was the guidance of divine revelation that led her to sing the songs of heroes and unite the suffering Minoans on the path that leads to where we are today.
Divine revelation represents your deepest desires. Believe in it, and it will guide your path and make you a hero.
Do you mean that Ithakos longed for death?
Nine years ago, Ithakos came to the temple and asked me if his dream of a river running red with blood was divine revelation.
I wanted to say no and tell him not to go, but I couldn't do it.
Divine revelation poured forth from my tongue, but I could not stop it or alter it, because it was not my place to deny someone else their most earnest desire.
As for Ithakos...
The blood and tears shed in the construction of the nomadic city of Athenius run deep in his bloodline. It was ever in his mind even when we served the temple together.
He sought a way out for his soul, a place beyond Athenius that he could build and protect.
But death was the inevitable price for this wish.
Perhaps you're telling the truth, but I have a hard time accepting it.
I know. Suffering rages in your blood, which pale words cannot soothe. Nothing I say can truly console you.
But I wish you could—*sigh*
It's been five years since you came to Athenius, five years since we met.
You spend most of your time at the river. You never come to the temple, and we rarely see each other except in front of Ithakos's grave.
I never needed the guidance of a priest.
But I was always watching you.

You were Ithakos's friend, the last trace that his life left behind. I could find some small comfort if you lived well—it was one little bit of selfishness that I allowed myself, outside of my duties as priestess.
I watched you acclimate to Athenius's rivers, watched you take in an apprentice, and watched the two of you go about your days together.
Lykeion is an honest, serious boy. It made my heart at ease that he was taking care of you.
Empty words could not comfort you. But what about these details of life? Have you not found comfort in it, even for an instant?
Was Athenius everything you thought it would be? There must have been something that was better than you expected, wasn't there?
...Maybe.
Then go think back to it, and think back to your past. And think about whether you've forgotten anything that was more important.
You need to live well... even if only for Ithakos's sake.
I hear something outside. I need to take a look.
The hour is near, Xanthos. Leave quickly, and avoid the crowds.
The temple's so big... how do I get to the audience seats of the theater from here...
That Corinian priest with a long name said he was 'going first'... or was it 'going away for a moment'? Is he coming back?
What do I do...?
What's that sound?
Moo—!
Huh?
Celery! Onion! Eggplant! Wait for me!
*pant* *pant*... Not a single burdenbeast responded to me the whole way from the ranch to the city... what's going on? Did something frighten you?
Moo—
Tomato! Were you waiting for me at the rear of the herd? Guess the extra care I gave you was worth it!
Just give me a moment to... catch my breath—
Mmph!

Oh, Tomato—
Tomato! Come back! Wait for me!
Moo—
There are a lot of people in the city. It's dangerous to run around like this.
Besides, it's rude to run and make noise in Kereseira's temple.
Moo.
That's right. Good one.
The burdenbeasts... they've stopped. All she did was gently put her fingers on the burdenbeast's head. Even Cauliflower, who's normally the grumpiest...
They say that even burdenbeasts could understand the music that Kereseira played on her harp. I always thought it was just a legend... convinced myself it must be...
I never thought I would actually meet someone who could talk to burdenbeasts for real!
Who... who are you? How do you talk to burdenbeasts? Can you teach me?
Talk? All you need to do is open your mouth... you can do it, can't you?
No, what I mean is, how do the burdenbeasts understand what you're saying—
They don't. At least, I don't think they do.
The girl lifts the finger that was on Cauliflower's forehead. The angry burdenbeast immediately lowers its shoulders and stomps its hooves, preparing to charge.
The girl puts the finger back.
You still have strength left. Impressive.
Are you the herder? Give me another minute. She'll settle down once she's out of energy.
The rest of the herd will calm down once the leader does.
So you just overpowered Cauliflower... with a single finger? So easily? It usually takes four or five strong guys to keep her down, and even that's not a guarantee...
Your name's Cauliflower? Nice name. It suits you well.
*Whine*...
Th-The tile beneath Cauliflower's feet is cracking!
Alright, that should do it.
The girl lets go, and Cauliflower's knees buckle as she lies down on the ground.
Then the girl does it to the rest of Cauliflower's herd, one by one, calming all the burdenbeasts in no time.

That's the last one. Huh?
What's that on your horn?
Thank you for your assistance, our distinguished guest, Cynisca, honored priestess of Lachedamon.
As for the harp you are holding, please give it to me.
It is the harp of Kereseira, belonging to the First Temple, which we have been waiting for.
Titi! Found you at last!
Did you follow the stampede of burdenbeasts to the temple? What happened at the ranch?
I... I just fell asleep at the ranch last night. Then I heard a huge commotion before Mio woke me up, and I saw that the harp was gone...
I saw the burdenbeasts running down the hill, so I followed them and ended up here... was it the burdenbeasts that brought the harp back to the square?
Yes... it's the miracle that we've been waiting for! The true harp of Kereseira!
Now, the only thing missing for the ceremony is the play...
Which isn't so hard. I memorized the Kereseira roles in all the plays. Once we settle on a program, we can find people to play the rest of the roles...
About that—I would like to select the play for this ceremony, in my capacity as the supervisor of the First Temple's completion.
Why are you so serious all of a sudden? What's the play you have in mind?
Kereseira and the Outlander.
You mean... the play that was banned for political reasons, and afterwards remained unperformed even after the ban was lifted because no Minoan wanted to play the role of the outlander?
Yes. It used to be the most popular classical play used in traditional ceremonies.
I will play the role of the outlander.
You don't need to do this. We all know how kind-hearted you are...
No. I want to do it.
I thought long and hard about it last night, and realized that this was my responsibility as a Sargonian, here and now.
Such beauty! The warm winds welcome the gentle waters of Delphi through the mountains.
Upon ruin and rubble we have built our temple. Now the sacred flame burns bright, in the fragrance of flowers and incense.
The songs and legends of heroes have given us courage to gather here this beautiful day—
Remember how greedy outlanders invaded our land, enslaved us, and silenced the song of all.
But our faith held strong. The outlanders could not break our will, nor chain our free souls.
We, the people of Minos formed in union, our faith and will victorious.
Now, we drive the last outlander from our soil!
As she recites the lines, Pallas seems to see the old First Temple and the mountain-rimmed gardens through Kereseira's own eyes.
Once, the people who had lost hearth and home gathered before Kereseira, just as the citizens of Athenius gather before Pallas now, sitting and standing.
They are all holding their breaths, waiting for the most important moment—

The moment that comforts the hearts of all Minoans and shows them that the long night and nightmare have passed, and the beautiful future that the divine revelations and heroic stories spoke of lies before them.
The last outlander is here.
Her heart beating wildly, Titi steps on stage, slowly but firmly.
This is what she has chosen, her play and her role.
There are words that she must say, things that she must do.
I repent for all that was done by us, our fathers, and their fathers before them.
We have trodden upon soil that was never ours—whether by choice or under the guise of compulsion—out of greed and baseness, out of fear and cowardice.
The hands that should have nurtured families tore the families of others apart. The spears that should have protected their own nation were pointed at innocents.
We must pay for all that we have done.
Your dagger, my lady.
Kereseira picks up the executional dagger.
The weapon has accompanied Kereseira ever since she hung her harp on the laurel tree.
Years of fighting have taken their toll in the form of chipping across the blade, but the tip remains sharp enough to take a life.
The outlander relaxes as Kereseira prepares to make her move.
As dramatic music begins to play, Pallas, playing Kereseira, hesitates a little.
Titi suddenly recalls the soldier who gave the blade to Kereseira. His face was covered, his figure unfamiliar, and his motions somewhat halting.
Was he one of the actors that she picked for the play? Titi begins to feel unsure.
The stage of the theater is wide and empty. The audience seems so far away, but Titi can hear their clamor. Are they excited about the play?

lines.st: —'Kill her! Make the invaders pay!'

lines.x: Or was it for the Sargonian standing before them in the theater?
She looks up at the dagger in Pallas's hand. The tip of the blade, which should have been made of rubber, reflects a metallic gleam in the sun.
All must end here.
Only then can we retrieve the harp from the laurel tree, drink fine wine, and return to the mountains and land that nourished us.
The play must go on. She must believe, believe in her friend, in the heroic spirit of the Minoans.
Titi closes her eyes.
Despicable! Disgrace!
Have you no pride, Minoans? Have you no honor?
Have you no heroic spirit?
The audience falls silent.
They slowly part to let a tall figure through, walking towards the stage with dignity and resolve.
He walks on stage, and kicks the Agamem soldier standing in his way to one side.
Swapping the dagger to make a murder look like an accident... since when did Minoans learn such underhanded methods?
Now THAT is what I wanted to see. A surprise, something unexpected.
Of course, it would have been interesting too if you hadn't held yourself back.
Athenius's business should be handled by the citizens of Athenius... we should trust Pallas, even if this man didn't make an appearance.
Please excuse my impetuousness.
Astynomos! What in the padishah's name is going on?!
It is an insult to have a Sargonian play such a—a denigrating role! And what did the man mean when he talked about the dagger? As an emissary of Sargon, I cannot allow—
Kindly collect yourself, Kýrios Behnui. It was Kyría Mashayer's own decision to play the role.
It is hardly becoming of an emissary to shout like this during such an important occasion, when the dust has yet to settle.
Xanthos...
So you never left Athenius...
Xanthos remembers the day when he raised his oar at the Sargonian he had tied to the tree.
But then he heard the horn of victory coming from the foothills, and the joyful Liberi brought news of the truce.
The frightened Sargonian stumbled and fell as he fled. Xanthos's comrades held him back and prevented him from killing the prisoner.
Slowly but surely, resentment began to build within his heart.
Life and vitality were gone from the soil of his homeland, but the marks that the Sargonians left behind linger.
Their banners, their landmines. The dam they built, which killed the river, became an indelible scar upon the land.
He saw many Sargonians make a hurried retreat towards the border on his way to Athenius.
He never joined the Minoans who threw rocks and spat at them, because it was not enough.
In Athenius, he rowed his ferry on the Delphi Canal day after day. He watched over the ebb and flow of the canal, but he could not keep the river of his own heart in check.
Rage roiled in his heart, seeking an opening.
Now, the opportunity that he had waited for so long has finally come.
He clutches his oar.
I, a criminal thrown into the death cell awaiting trial, have waited an eternity for this moment.
No matter what punishment awaits me for all these reasons—
I will stand before the Sargonians and my own people, with nothing to hide, and ask my question of you!
The audience is hushed. Even the haughty Sargonian deputy falls silent.
I lost my home and my dearest friend in the war.
I followed in his tracks and saw countless of our own at death's door along the way.
The dreams we once had were trampled underfoot by the invaders, never to be rebuilt.
Why, I ask the invaders, did you set foot on this land that did not belong to you?
And what makes you think you can shamelessly come to Athenius just a few years after the truce, acting like nothing ever happened?
He looks up at the two people in the middle of the stage. The Sargonian woman opens her mouth, but closes it without saying anything.
Pallas lowers the dagger.
My people, I am surprised how quickly you forget.
Did a century of colonization and oppression leave nothing behind in your hearts?
The Sargonians destroyed our homes and cities, distorted our culture, extinguished our language.
Is this memory not enough for you to be on guard against the Sargonians?
How could you cast the past aside? How dare you continue to deal with them?
Culture? Exchange? Fine words!
They're not just taking the technical data from the construction of the Three Poleis, but the blood and sweat that we shed when we built them!
They're taking away evidence of their crimes, rewriting history, creating an illusion of Sargonian friendship to wipe away the memory!
All of you are accomplices and accessories!
Xanthos looks towards the audience. The Sargonian deputy is furious, while the Astynomos of Athenius is utterly impassive, as though she fully expected him to be here.
Kassandra is looking at him with a worried expression a short distance away, while his apprentice, Lykeion, is watching from the shadows in a distant corner alongside his old classmate.
The two people on stage look stunned. He does not know how much of it is an act, and how much of it is his own imagination.
Lying beneath their feet is the soldier whom he kicked aside, looking equally surprised.
Yes, the war is over. It seems like we could forget all the hurt and pain, and begin a new life.
So we dug out the divine revelations, dramas and legends from old paper piles, built temples and altars, and thought we were continuing the legacy of the heroes.
Does a life of leisure really bring inner peace? Do pale words really comfort the departed?
I see only conspiracy, scheming and selfish desires. Where is the spirit of Minos? Where is true heroic spirit?
All is silent. Then, the Sargonian woman steps forward.
I can see the frustration and yearning in your eyes.
You want to cross swords with the Sargonians, face to face. This is your heroic spirit.
Here is your chance. Come. As a Sargonian, I owe you this battle.
(It's been a long time since I last felt hot blood flowing in my veins.)
(Not since I left the battlefield and left my homeland have I...)
His vision begins to blur from the loss of blood. Everything seems covered in a white glow, and he instinctively closes his eyes.
He hears the song of a harp, but he does not know from whence it comes.
He forces his eyes open, and sees himself in an unfamiliar space. He seems to be floating in mid-air, people passing in front of his eyes without acknowledging him.
It is as though he is in a dream.
I will never work with the Sargonians! Beat me, torture me, kill me if you want—
But you won't get one word out of me about the guerrillas!
So there are guerrillas.
...Skata!
Why are you so angry? I thought Minoans liked logic. That's what the stories of heroes all say.
Are you trying to butter me up? I'm not talking!
No need to be so on edge. Just wanted to have a conversation. I don't like interrogating people either.
But I'll be reprimanded if I don't spend enough time in here. Nothing I can do.
What are you doing, Mashayer? Are you so stupid you can't do something as simple as interrogate a prisoner?
I'm on it, sir!
Piece of trash. Some noble. Pah.
The prisoner watches as his interrogator jumps up from the ground nervously, while his superior leaves after casually hurling a few words of abuse.
Both seem used to such a situation. It is as though the officer cusses and abuses the guard on a daily basis.
The guard's complexion is young and pale. He is thin and mild-mannered, very different from the villagers' impression of Sargonians.
The prisoner warns himself not to let his guard down.
Sargonians are cunning. Perhaps the two of them conspired to put on an act.
Well, I have to act like I'm interrogating you.
Could you... say something? Anything.
Anything? I've got a litany of curses for you. I could go on for days.
...Let's talk about something else. Something nicer, something that can help us forget about our struggles and helplessness... tell me some Minoan heroic stories.
I know some of your legends from ancient books that I collected, but they're not easy to find in Sargon.
You already told this story a couple of days ago. I remember.
Tsk.
Anything else? Think!
(Weren't we just putting on a show for his superior? Why is he taking such careful notes?)
(Why would a Sargonian love Minoan legends so much? Is there something not right with this guy's head?)
You've only told a hundred or so. There must be more stories and legends in the land of heroes.
(Trying to provoke me? Not falling for that!)
Fragments you heard from the elders as a child are fine. It doesn't need to be complete or popular.
The kind of story that would make a little Sargonian girl dream about Minos... there must be more, right?
(Skata... well, if that's what he wants...)
(I'll just make one up. It's not like this idiotis Sargonian could tell.)
Alright. Let me tell you about Siopi, the silent hero.
It is the strangest experience the prisoner has ever had. No one would believe the story if he told it. He decided to take it with him to his grave—
If he ever gets out of here.
Bloody bother. Finally got him off my back for the day.

I didn't know I was so good at making up stories. I should become a bard if I ever get out.
If I ever get out...
Is that a crack in the door?
Did that ilithios forget to lock the door?
It has been a long time since he last saw the stars. It almost feels like he is dreaming as he breathes the air of freedom again.
Is he out? Is he free? Just like this?
Useless trash! You've been in that Minoan's cell every single day, and you haven't gotten one scrap of intel about the guerrillas?
S-Sorry, sir. I've been taking records, but he contradicts himself every day. I... I'm afraid nothing he says can be trusted.
I knew you couldn't get anything done! Incompetent fool!
I'll interrogate him tomorrow while you watch and learn how things are done in the lord ameer's army!
Y-Yes, sir...
The fugitive hears the guard and his superior talking in the distance. He hastens his movement through the bushes.
The movement seems to have drawn the attention of the Sargonians.
How did the prisoner get out?!
After him, Mashayer, you worthless shit! You are DEAD, if you don't catch him!
Hearing the sound made by the skinny guard's ill-fitting armor sets the fugitive's heart at ease.
He knows a single Sargonian could never catch him, on this Minoan land.
He takes one final look back as he escapes the Sargonian camp.
The fugitive sees the frail guard stumble and fall. The guard's luxurious armor, probably an heirloom, weighs him down so much that he struggles to get himself off the ground.
Or perhaps... he just wanted an excuse to not get up?
Did he really forget to lock the door, or did he finally find an opportunity to leave a crack?
The prisoner does not know.
The only thing that the prisoner knows is that he will never forget the time he spent at the Sargonian camp, and the ridiculous-looking Sargonian.
(What... is this?)
It's my father.
Xanthos turns around in surprise, and sees the Sargonian woman, standing in the same vision as he.
There is surprise on her face, yes, but also nostalgia and poignance.

I know he came to Minos in the lord ameer's army, but was sent back to Sargon for his incompetence. He would have received a heavier punishment, but what little glory the House of Mashayer had left protected him one last time.
He never really... achieved anything, and it wasn't a surprise that he would mess up after he was conscripted. He didn't like to talk about his time in Minos, and I never asked...
I didn't know this was where those stories of the Minoan heroes he told me came from.
He's very different from my image of a Sargonian.
Yes. He was always weak and soft, never talking back even when insulted to his face... I always wondered why he loved heroic stories so much, when he was the furthest thing from a hero.
No wonder... no wonder he told me the story of Siopi the silent hero so many times, the hero who kept a secret for thirty years...
He planted the seed of heroes in your heart.
I have high hopes for you, Medjehtiqedti Nasutu Mashayer.
It is an honor.
Do you understand why I appointed someone like you to lead the mission, someone so young and inexperienced?
You must have your reasons.
You're much smarter than your father was.
The House of Mashayer was a noble house with a rich tradition, and I was on good terms with your elders.
It pained me to see the family fall on hard times under your father, who was frail and idle.
I arranged a cushy post for him in the lord ameer's army in Minos, safely away from the frontlines, and he almost got himself into a court-martial.
When Minos came up again after all these years, you were the first person I thought of.
Young Medjehtiqedti is much sharper than her father, I thought. I'm sure she will have no trouble bringing a harp to Minos and bringing back a few blueprints that belonged to Sargon to begin with.
You'll do a fine job and do your family proud, won't you?
Yes, exalted padishah! I loved the legends of Minos, and I've wanted to see true heroism with my own eyes since I was little. I'll make sure the Minoan mission goes perfectly!
The padishah said a bunch of flowery words to me before I left, but I never understood why he made me the head of this mission.
I guess it's because it's easy to get rid of me, if something goes wrong.
And I had no idea that those 'blueprints' contained such detailed records.
Frankly, I don't really care about doing the Mashayer name proud.
I came because I wanted to see this land that I've always been fascinated by with my own eyes, to chase the legends that I've only heard about...
Or perhaps, I wanted to find some answers about my father...
What is this land of heroes that father talked about really like, and what is the heroic spirit really like? I wanted to learn, to find out. That's why I came to Minos.
Have you found the answer?
Yes. What about you?
I was once like you, Xanthos.
Pallas?! You too...
I've seen with my own eyes how our people died shielding their comrades from the Sargonians, and heard the screaming of the wounded being amputated in village huts.
Hate flowed in your heart and mine, in the hearts of every Minoan.
Even now... in my heart...
I know.

But have you ever stopped to think why we love to drink?
Besides the sweet fragrance of wine and the joy of harvest that it represents, the inspiration and joy that it brings us...
Besides all that...
It's also because it makes us forget, if only for a while.
Too many things trouble us and make us hurt, but we can't forget them completely, because to do so would be a betrayal, so we drown ourselves in drink, seeking a moment's forgetfulness.
But then... I had to leave Minos.
You're Pallas, aren't you? The medics told me you often drink here.
Yes, I am... what is it?
You're Minoan, right? You've been in the Lachedamon competitions? Do you know Dikaiopolis, the Blood Knight? Did you ever fight him?
No... but I've heard of him. A Minoan Infected who participates in the Kazimierz Majors, I think?
Well, actually, the 'competitions' in those two places are not quite the same thing...
But they're both clashes of physical ability and skill, aren't they?
Mm... well... uh...
I knew it! The principles of strength are the same everywhere! The Blood Knight must be strong because of his Minoan upbringing!
I wanted to meet the Blood Knight, but he's retired now.
Still, it's an honor to meet another Minoan master! Can I have your autograph? Anywhere's fine—how about my t-shirt?
...(Quietly signs)
Thank you so much!
So... do Minoans have a special way of training?
I heard you love drinking. Is this part of your training? Accelerating blood circulation with alcohol and increasing explosiveness?
The medics also say you can fall asleep anywhere! This must be a way to save energy! You only fully utilize your physical ability when necessary!
I see! This is why the Blood Knight was so explosive!
Er... these are just my personal habits. I don't know how the Blood Knight trains, but I doubt...
I get it, I get it, just fantasizing about things. Did I get carried away? I hope I didn't cause any offense.
...It's alright. You really love Dikaiopolis, don't you?
Yeah! I lost all hope after learning that I have Oripathy, but the Blood Knight gave me the will to live again.
I watched recordings of his bouts so many times! I've even started training myself, hoping to join an Infected Knightclub someday.
He was the one who blazed the trail for the Infected in Kazimierzian knightsports, paving the way for the Radiant Knight's return to the tournaments!
His bout with the Radiant Knight was one for the ages! I was there for it, and I cried when they took the podium together.
I wasn't sure why I cried. Maybe it's because I saw a flicker of hope for the future.
I guess he's the kind of hero the Minoans talk about!
He's Infected. According to conventional Minoan wisdom...
Do Infected heroes have some kind of special title in Minos?
No... But he's a hero, yes. No one can deny it.
Traditions don't stay unchanged forever, Xanthos.
The stories of the heroes defined and shaped who we are. Yet, because heroes arise from among us, we in turn possess the power to define them and to reshape what it means to be a hero.
I learned that after I left Minos.
Our goddess of victory is back!
I heard you destroyed another Sargon cavalry unit that tried to sneak across the border?
I told you not to call me that when we're in the village... it's embarrassing.
It was the title that we gave to Pallas...
Whoever leads us to victory is our goddess of victory. That's what Pallas said when she left—
'You can use this overblown title however you want, if it scares the Sargonians.'
As long as we still have our 'Goddess of Victory', you'll keep the Sargonians away.
Yeah... although Sargonian raiders still come every now and then, since she signed that 'Non-Aggression Pact' with Sargon's border tribes on behalf of the villages in the area...
The raids have been getting fewer and fewer after we won a few victories. It's all thanks to the reputation of the 'Goddess of Victory'.
Besides, inheriting Pallas's title makes me feel like I can't give up, which helps me pull through in tough battles.
It doesn't just scare our enemies; it also inspires our own.
Yeah, that's what heroes are for.
The village of Akroti... brings back so many memories.
It's very much like my homeland.
All the villages of Minos are alike. The same soil nurtured us.
But it's not just because of the same soil that we're a single nation. It's because we know the same stories.
Only we, upon seeing the olive branch, remember how the hero Pheidippides ran from Athenius to spread the news of our victory over the Sargonians to the other cities.
He was carrying the olive branch of Athenius, and so it became the symbol of victory and hope to us.
This is what truly belongs to us, something that outlanders and invaders cannot take away.
As long as we keep living, we'll carry on these stories, and change the traditions so that the legends of Minos's heroes may continue, that the spirit of Minos may continue.
Minos is wherever we are.
It is a strange scene that unfolds before the eyes of the citizens of Athenius and the guests from Sargon and other city-states.
It is an exhilarating battle. But when the oar-wielding Minoan warrior's blood falls upon the strings of the harp in the hands of Kereseira's statue—
The ancient harp begins to make a sound, like an old machine starting up after a long slumber, its gears slowly beginning to turn.
As the harp begins to sing, the trio on the stage slows down, entering a trance-like state, sometimes angry, sometimes sad, sometimes laughing, sometimes weeping.
When the Minoan challenger is finally overcome by exhaustion and slumps down by the statue's pedestal, the other two on stage finally break out of their trance.
They look around themselves in confusion before running towards the challenger, reaching out to him to try to help him up, but getting no response from him.
The shouting, the cheering, and the angry yelling have all ceased. Those present begin to realize that they have witnessed a miracle.
The harp that stopped singing after the Sargonians invaded has returned to where it belonged, and declared—
The tradition continues, and a new chapter is written in the stories of the heroes.
The harp's music echoes in the sky above the square.
Xanthos looks up.
The autumn sun is painfully bright, but for some reason it seems to bring back dim memories, back to the day that he said goodbye to Ithakos.

lines.st: Do you believe that the guerrillas can defeat the Sargonians?
Even though we've been losing for a century?

lines.x: (Nods)
I haven't received any divine revelations, and I can't tell the future. I can't even leave with you.
But I will promise what I can.
I'll protect our village, our houses, our farms and flowerbeds. Perhaps we can go to Athenius, where you came from, once you're victorious.

I'll still be a ferryman, ferrying you to your workplace at the temple on the other side of the city as the sun shines upon the Delphi Canal... I've been imagining the scene ever since you described it to me.
...Are you listening, Ithakos?

lines.st: Minos is in for a cold winter this year, brother.

lines.x: Yes. But the spring will be all the warmer for it.
He finally remembers what he said to Ithakos.
And he remembers the warm smile on Ithakos's face at the answer.
The pain and anger of losing a dear friend had clouded his memory and caused him to forget what was most important.
But things will get better, when spring comes and war is over, when life gets back on track.
The wounds of the earth will heal, and the snowmelt from the peaks of Heria will continue to flow, bringing flowers and reeds home.
Medication and suppressants that need to be administered on a regular basis, fresh from the Rhodes Island office.
I heard the Astynomos' Office has lifted the detention order on Xanthos and his apprentice, and does not intend to press charges against you for helping them escape.
Yes. I learned later that Lydia had guessed our plan and intentionally made things easy for me. Stands to reason she wouldn't pursue the matter.
But the three of you still intend to leave Athenius?
Yes.
Staying will only give my old classmate ideas. He needs to see more places, meet more people, do more things.
Yes.
I rarely see you so honest, Lykeion. I wouldn't have had three operators keep an eye on you, if you were like this when you were my patient.
Sorry for the trouble I caused...
Looks like Xanthos's actions at the First Temple had a considerable influence on you.
How about you wave the white flag and admit that Oripathy doesn't sap the heroic spirit at all?
In your dreams.
Talk about a hardhead.
You should go, or you'll miss the ferry. Xanthos's substitute is not as flexible about late passengers. He won't wait for you.

Skata! Let's go, Lykeion.
Thank you for the medicine, Kyría Athopoulou. May we meet again!

Hey, stay off the lawn! I just worked on it—
Well, I suppose two young men won't be nearly as destructive as a whole herd of burdenbeasts.
The flowers and grass they ruined haven't been cleaned up yet. Too bad about the blue boronia...
Don't cry, Lydia...
If bad guys ruin your house like they ruined my old home...
We won't have a home to go back to. What would we do?
Don't worry. I'll never leave you behind, even if the house is gone, even if we're separated!
I know! Look for this garden if you're far away from home and don't know the way back.
Garden...?
Yeah! I'll plant blue boronia here, so that you'll know you're home when you see it.
And if you find the way first, mark it the same way, won't you?
Alright... it's a promise, Lena.
The public garden's lawn, once filled with flowers and herbs, is now filled only with burdenbeast footprints.
Even if it has temporarily lost its beauty, however, the blue petals remain in the soil.
So many blue boronias... you think of the past too, don't you?
Thank you, Lydia. I'm home.
I brought this year's apples, Kassandra!
The portion set aside for the temple's offering has been sent to the cellar. I saved this for you and the others at the temple to share.
Thank you. It's very kind of you, to do it every year.
Our good harvests are all thanks to the help of everyone in Athenius. Besides, you helped so much with coordinating the temple.
You seem to be in a good mood today.
To us, nothing matters more than the harvest, and a good harvest brings happiness.
Nothing matters to us more than everybody's happiness, and the harvest is an important part of it.
We're counting on you! I'll take my leave now. The pomegranate will be ripe for harvest soon, and there are branches waiting to be trimmed.
Sweet apples and bright sun. What a lovely day!
The ceremony at the First Temple was successfully concluded, and the ferryman that you were caring for has found hope.
All is well, isn't it, Hiéreia? Why do you look so sullen?
You're the one I'm worried about.
Me? Why?
I've been thinking about what you said when you came back to Athenius.
You said you saw the beginnings of Minos in your dreams, saw Kereseira and her First Temple.
You said you thought a lot about Minos's past and future, about your own goals and direction.
All this is why you came back.
The events of recent days seem to corroborate my interpretation of the divine revelation. It is not a beautiful future of smiles and flowers. The unease lingers in my heart.
Divine revelation is simply the direction that the spirit of heroes has shown us. It is not an inevitable future. We learned about that in priest school.
That's true for ordinary revelations, but...
For example, I wanted the harp to return, but the peculiar manner of its return makes it seem so inevitable and unchangeable...
Look at it from a different perspective. While the final goal was the return of the harp, the manner of its return was wondrous. Perhaps it's the same for my revelation.
Or perhaps, your interpretation is not the final goal. It's only one of the trials during the process, and the goal lies elsewhere.
...I hope you're right.
Besides, the recent events have demonstrated that it was right for me to come back. Minos needs me, and I need it.
But you don't want to take over the First Temple, despite the fact that the bulk of the work is done.
Is it because you believe your Oripathy will be an obstacle to priesthood? But Xanthos has told me about the vision he saw on the temple square. I thought you didn't worry anymore.
Traditions can be changed. You were right. I can initiate a vote at the council...
No, I didn't propose rebuilding the First Temple to become its priestess.
I wanted to unite our spirit, not just Athenians, but other city-states and Minoans too...
I heard Lachedamon and Corinia have changed a lot. I want to see them for myself.
So you did come to say goodbye.
Not quite yet. I want to see the temple project through to the end. I'm not missing the completion party that Kantia is already planning!
Carefree as always.
Thank you for the kind invitation. It was quite an unexpected play.
Athenius is about to depart along its planned route, and it's time for us to depart as well.
I hope you will excuse the unpredictable events that occurred. It seems I did not plan for every possible contingency.
On this, there is something I'm curious about.
The ferryman openly accused you of making secret deals with the Sargonians on the temple square, and you were summoned to the council yesterday for an emergency hearing.
Yet you are back in your office, safe and sound. Did you have a trump card up your sleeve? Or is there more to this matter than meets the eye?

More than meets the eye' is overstating it. I had simply made certain arrangements for the follow-up to this matter. The council heard the explanation and is satisfied that I remain competent to govern.
As for the details, I regret that I am unable to share them as yet. They will reveal themselves in due time.
All we need to know is that you remain the Astynomos of Athenius.
With sweeping authority over major matters of administration and governance.
I'm glad to hear it. And now, I have a formal proposal to make.
I'm sure both of you understand that dramatic changes are happening in Minos right now.
It is the case in Athenius, and it is the case in Lachedamon, where a new arena is being built, one that will host competitors from far and wide.
Perhaps the word 'tradition' is not quite music to your ears, Astynomos, but don't you think that now is the time for us to revive the old traditions?
Changes are happening across the land. How about the Three Poleis set aside their rivalries, return to the old city-state federation, and revive an ancient festival?
The scattered villages and minor poleis will surely answer the call, and the nation will grow stronger.
It will be the perfect opportunity, to restore Minos's glory, and to spread the sacred flame across the land.
What do you think?
Is this why you've been snooping around Athenius? You were investigating us.
Old merchant habits die hard. Can't go into the market without doing research. I apologize if I caused any offense.
You're good at causing accidents. I'm afraid I can't give you an answer right now.
That's not a problem. It's right to be prudent.
What about you, priestess Cynisca of Lachedamon?
I cannot make decisions for Lachedamon by myself. However, there is one thing of which I'm sure—
Lachedamon never turns down a challenge.
I'll take you across the river, then you should go directly back to Sargon.

Man, I feel like a prisoner being deported—
Just kidding.
I understand your friendship towards Minos. But you are still Sargonian, nonetheless.
And I have a hard time abiding Sargonian feet upon the soil of Minos.
...I understand.

I've been thinking whether there's anything else I can do for Minos, for the mistakes of my forefathers.
Whether I should leave the engineering notes of the Three Poleis and bring only the blueprints back—
After all, the padishah only told me to bring back 'blueprints'.
Then I thought, it wasn't enough—it's just a half-hearted compromise, an excuse to run away from my responsibilities and lessen the feeling of guilt.
I should bring them back and display them in my museum, so that the Sargonians can see it and know that their history books are lying.
Your padishah won't be happy about it.
Who cares? My family wasn't ever particularly good at making the padishahs happy.
Besides, I met with your Astynomos, that shrewd Vulpo, yesterday. I learned that she had already made preparations in advance.
She had scholars compile and photocopy all the documents and even wrote a paper on it, to be published in a Columbian journal once the exchange was complete and I brought the documents back.
She asked me to write a foreword, from the perspective of a Sargonian, providing endorsement for the paper.
And she wanted to leverage the connections I built while studying in Columbia to invite experts to study these invaluable historical records.
She said she would name me an honorary Minoan for this!
...I admire your courage.
Just doing what my father couldn't.
Besides, I enjoyed my time in Athenius. I went to see the priestess of Endymion Temple yesterday, and she said the vision I saw on stage was a divine revelation!
It was a surprise, because the books said Minoan divine revelations only happened in dreams.

Divine revelations can occur in the form of dreams, visions or voices, but dreams are the most frequent form they take, because they are an important way for us to interact with the spirits of the heroes and the departed.
That's what the priestess said. I told her about the vision I had on the stage, and the dream I had at the burdenbeast ranch. She gave me a very detailed analysis.
Finally she told me, in that solemn and mysterious tone, 'You may not believe it now, Kyría Mashayer, but I believe your revelation is a sign that you will one day become a padishah.'
How did she get that? She wasn't messing with me, was she?
I'm an honorary Minoan now. I'll actually believe what the priestess of Endymion tells me!
...You're free to believe. Divine revelations are simply a form of guidance. Believe in it, and it will bring you strength.
Perhaps a lot of tragedies could have been avoided if a padishah like you ruled over the Sargonian border.

Hm, I think I understand now.
Then I'll take it as a sign of the responsibility I may have to bear in the future—I'll do my best.
This public announcement... is it a referendum?
Yes.
I'm going back to Athenius after I take you across the river.
The citizens of Athenius will vote on whether to banish me from the city for my recent actions.
But you did it for Minos—
Regardless of the reason, I must face judgment for my actions. It is the responsibility I must bear.
It's time. Let's go, Kyría Mashayer.
I saw Ithakos again in the vision, as well as the river of my homeland.
Was it still red with blood, as it was in your dreams?
No, it was quiet and gentle, as I remembered it; not pristine, but carrying the sand and mud that nourishes the soil and life.
A beautiful revelation. You'll be able to take up poems again, no matter where you go.
...Yes.
Whatever the result of the referendum, whether he stays in or leaves Athenius, Xanthos will accept it with grace.
He walks towards the square.
Three months later...

Look at this letter from a Lateran scholar—'A most stunning revelation. The study of history is a continuous process of seeking the truth by eliminating misconceptions. I am impressed by the courage that your nation has demonstrated.'
And this editorial in a Columbian newspaper—'Sargon shows integrity through proper recognition of history.'
I've been calling in favors every single day to repair the damage you've done, ever since that special exhibit that you held after getting back from Minos!
I hope you understand that such maverick methods only work once.
If anything like this happens again, you'd better think about what you'll do once you're removed as curator of the Varjavandabad Museum.
Is this your will, or the padishah's?
Is there a difference? My will is the padishah's will.
I thought we had a consensus before we left Minos—
The success of the exchange ceremony and the mission of friendship with Minos is all thanks to your leadership. In turn, it will be your responsibility to explain things to the padishah.
However much or little you say is entirely up to you, as long as the padishah's happy.
The exchange ceremony was completed under my leadership to begin with! Besides, how was I to know that you would be so audacious as to...!
Don't think you're in the clear just because I smoothed things over for you this time. There won't be a next time.
Besides, and don't you dare say I didn't warn you—this matter has had a significant impact far and wide. My connections have told me that the City of Gold has sent a herald to Menat-Ha'mait.
You WILL watch your words and behavior from now on.
I see that news of my mission precedes me.
S-Such noble elegance and dignity! You must be the herald of the Shahanshah!
Your presence honors this humble museum. Have you come to see the exhibits?
If you want to see the new special Minoan exhibit, I can...
You stay put! I will guide the exalted herald.
The exhibit can wait. I came to communicate the Shahanshah's will.
His Majesty has heard of the mission from Menat-Ha'mait to Minos, and he is most interested.
Y-You came to escort me? I-It's my greatest honor!
My family is one of the most honored houses in this land. It was always my greatest dream to serve the Shahanshah in the City of Gold. I knew that one day I would...
It's not you I seek, effendi. Kindly close your mouth, for it is making too much noise.
Medjehtiqedti Nasutu Mashayer. His Majesty awaits your presence in the City of Gold, that the King of Kings might hear of your time in Minos, in your own words.
You should make your preparations quickly. The Shahanshah is eager to hear from you. Do not keep him waiting.
Of course!
